Attempts — Key Cases.application: D jumped onto a racecourse to distract greyhounds and cause a race to be declared void, so he could recover a losing bet. The CA held D had not yet embarked upon the crime of theft — he had not yet gone to the bookmaker to claim the money back.
Attempts — Key Cases.conclusion: Gullefer defines the threshold for attempt as 'embarking on the crime itself'. Compared with R v Jones (Kenneth) [1990] — where D pointing a loaded gun at close range had crossed the line — Gullefer's acts were still entirely preparatory. Both cases must be read together.
Attempts — Key Cases.summary_line: R V GULLEFER 1990 · ATTEMPT · MORE THAN MERELY PREPARATORY · EMBARKED ON CRIME PROPER · OBJECTIVE TEST

Attempts — Key Cases.issue: Whether D could be convicted of attempting to handle a controlled drug where the substance was in fact harmless vegetable matter.
Attempts — Key Cases.rule: A person may be convicted of attempting to commit an offence even where commission of the full offence is impossible because the relevant facts do not exist. Under s.1(2)–(3) CAA 1981, impossibility is no defence to attempt.
Attempts — Key Cases.application: D was caught carrying a suitcase he believed contained heroin. The substance was in fact harmless. The HL held D guilty — overruling Anderton v Ryan. D's intention and acts were sufficient for attempt regardless of the actual nature of the substance.
Attempts — Key Cases.conclusion: Shivpuri definitively resolved the impossibility question: impossibility is never a defence to attempt. Overruled Anderton v Ryan — one of the few cases the HL overruled within a year of deciding. Note the contrast with statutory conspiracy under R v Nock [1978].
Attempts — Key Cases.summary_line: R V SHIVPURI 1987 · ATTEMPTING THE IMPOSSIBLE · S.1(2) CAA 1981 · FACTS AS BELIEVED · HL OVERRULED ANDERTON V RYAN

Scenario Cards — Inchoate.front: Vince plans to rob a bank. He researches the bank's layout online, withdraws cash for expenses, and purchases a balaclava and zip ties. He is arrested at home before leaving for the bank. Is Vince guilty of attempted robbery?
Scenario Cards — Inchoate.answer: Probably not at this stage. Criminal Attempts Act 1981 s.1(1) requires that D does an act which is more than merely preparatory to the commission of the offence. The test is whether D has moved from preparation into the execution phase (R v Gullefer [1990]). Purchasing equipment and conducting research are acts of preparation. The Rubicon has not been crossed — Vince has not yet embarked on the crime itself. Compare R v Jones [1990] (getting into a car with a loaded gun pointed at the victim — sufficient) and R v Campbell [1991] (standing outside a post office with robbery equipment — insufficient). On these facts, Vince is still in the preparatory stage. He could be charged with conspiracy if others are involved, or with possession offences in relation to the equipment.
Scenario Cards — Inchoate.summary_line: ATTEMPT · CAA 1981 S.1 · MORE THAN MERELY PREPARATORY · GULLEFER · JONES · CAMPBELL · PREPARATION VS EXECUTION

Scenario Cards — Inchoate.front: Wendy agrees with Xavier and Yusuf to import cocaine. Before any cocaine is imported, Wendy withdraws from the agreement and tells Xavier and Yusuf she wants nothing more to do with it. Xavier and Yusuf proceed without her. Is Wendy guilty of conspiracy?
Scenario Cards — Inchoate.answer: Yes, Wendy is guilty of statutory conspiracy under s.1(1) Criminal Law Act 1977. Conspiracy is complete at the moment of agreement — the offence is committed when two or more persons agree to pursue a course of conduct that will necessarily involve a criminal offence. Wendy agreed with Xavier and Yusuf to import cocaine: the conspiracy was complete at that point. Withdrawal after the agreement is formed does not retrospectively undo the offence of conspiracy (R v Gortat [1973]). Wendy's withdrawal may be relevant to sentencing and may affect whether she is liable for any subsequent offences committed by Xavier and Yusuf as secondary party, but it does not affect her guilt of the conspiracy itself.
Scenario Cards — Inchoate.summary_line: CONSPIRACY · CLA 1977 S.1 · COMPLETE ON AGREEMENT · WITHDRAWAL NO DEFENCE · R V GORTAT · SENTENCING RELEVANCE ONLY

Scenario Cards — Inchoate.front: Zara sends an anonymous email to her colleague Adam telling him that their mutual enemy Ben 'deserves what is coming to him' and that 'someone should teach him a lesson.' Adam reads the email and decides, entirely independently, to assault Ben. Zara is charged with encouraging or assisting the offence under the Serious Crime Act 2007. Advise.
Scenario Cards — Inchoate.answer:
Zara may be liable under s.44 Serious Crime Act 2007 (intentionally encouraging or assisting an offence) or s.45 (encouraging or assisting believing the offence will be committed). The key issues are:

1. Did Zara's email amount to encouragement? Vague language ('deserves what is coming') may be insufficient — there must be an act capable of encouraging the specific offence (s.65 SCA 2007 defines 'encouraging').
2. Did Zara intend to encourage an assault, or merely express frustration? Intention to encourage is required under s.44.
3. The fact that Adam acted independently does not automatically sever the link — the SCA 2007 does not require the principal to have been encouraged; it requires that D's act was capable of encouraging.

On these facts the ambiguity of the language and absence of a specific request make conviction uncertain.

⚠️ This is a developing area — the SCA 2007 offences have generated limited appellate guidance.
Scenario Cards — Inchoate.summary_line: ENCOURAGING/ASSISTING · SCA 2007 SS.44-45 · INTENTIONAL ENCOURAGEMENT · AMBIGUOUS LANGUAGE · CAPABILITY TO ENCOURAGE · INDEPENDENT ACTION OF PRINCIPAL

Principal & Secondary Liability.front: What is the distinction between a principal and a secondary party (accessory) in criminal law?
Principal & Secondary Liability.answer:
A principal directly brings about the AR. A secondary party aids, abets, counsels, or procures the commission of the offence: s.8 Accessories and Abettors Act 1861.
Meaning:

- Aid: assist before or during

- Abet: encourage at the time of the offence

- Counsel: advise or instruct beforehand

- Procure: bring about by effort — need not be present: A-G's Reference (No 1 of 1975) [1975]
Both equally liable: s.8 AAA 1861.

⚠️ Innocent agency — using an innocent person as instrument — makes D the principal, not an accessory.
Principal & Secondary Liability.summary_line: PRINCIPAL · SECONDARY PARTY · ACCESSORY · AIDING · ABETTING · COUNSELLING · PROCURING · S.8 ACCESSORIES AND ABETTORS ACT 1861

Principal & Secondary Liability.front: What is the mens rea required for secondary liability?
Principal & Secondary Liability.answer:
The secondary party must:
1. Intend to do the acts of assistance or encouragement; AND
2. Know the essential matters constituting the principal's offence — knowledge of the type of offence planned: R v Bainbridge [1960].
Knowledge of the type of crime is required — not mere suspicion: Johnson v Youden [1950]. Wilful blindness may satisfy the knowledge requirement.

⚠️ Exam trap: suspicion is not enough. The accessory must know the type of offence the principal intends to commit.
Principal & Secondary Liability.summary_line: SECONDARY LIABILITY MR · KNOWLEDGE OF ESSENTIAL MATTERS · INTENTION TO ASSIST OR ENCOURAGE · R V BAINBRIDGE · A-G'S REFERENCE NO 1

Principal & Secondary Liability.front: What is the effect of R v Jogee [2016] on joint enterprise and parasitic accessory liability?
Principal & Secondary Liability.answer:
R v Jogee [2016] (SC) overruled Chan Wing-Siu and held that:
1. Foresight alone is insufficient — the accessory must intend to assist or encourage the principal to commit the collateral offence
2. Foresight is only evidence from which intention may be inferred
Post-Jogee: D must have intentionally assisted/encouraged the commission of the specific offence actually committed by the principal.

⚠️ The change was retrospective — but the bar for allowing fresh appeals is high: R v Johnson and others [2016].
Principal & Secondary Liability.summary_line: R V JOGEE · JOINT ENTERPRISE · PARASITIC ACCESSORY LIABILITY ABOLISHED · FORESIGHT NOT SUFFICIENT · INTENTION TO ASSIST REQUIRED

Principal & Secondary Liability.front: When can a secondary party withdraw from a joint enterprise to escape liability?
Principal & Secondary Liability.answer:
Withdrawal must be timely and effective before the offence is committed:
1. Before the crime begins: unequivocal communication of withdrawal may suffice: R v Becerra [1975].
2. Once the crime is underway: D may need to neutralise the assistance already given: R v Rook [1993].
3. Spontaneous departure: where the principal acts in a way fundamentally different from what was agreed, secondary party may not be liable: R v Mitchell [1999].

⚠️ Merely walking away or regretting involvement is not enough if the assistance already given remains operative.
Principal & Secondary Liability.summary_line: WITHDRAWAL · TIMELY AND EFFECTIVE · COMMUNICATION TO PRINCIPAL · SPONTANEOUS VIOLENCE EXCEPTION · R V BECERRA

Principal & Secondary Liability.front: When does mere presence at the scene of a crime make a person liable as an accessory?
Principal & Secondary Liability.answer:
Mere presence at the scene is not without more sufficient to constitute aiding and abetting: R v Coney [1882]. Presence becomes criminal participation only where:
1. D's presence was by prior agreement intended to encourage the principal: R v Allan [1965]
2. D's presence gives intentional encouragement communicated to the principal: R v Clarkson [1971]
3. D has a duty to intervene and deliberately chooses not to

⚠️ Exam trap: presence + knowledge that a crime is being committed ≠ liability. Intentional encouragement communicated to the principal is the threshold.
Principal & Secondary Liability.summary_line: MERE PRESENCE · NOT SUFFICIENT FOR SECONDARY LIABILITY · ENCOURAGEMENT REQUIRED · R V CONEY · KNOWLEDGE AND VOLUNTARY PRESENCE

Principal & Secondary Liability.front: Can a secondary party be convicted where the principal has been acquitted?
Principal & Secondary Liability.answer:
Yes. The acquittal of the principal does not automatically prevent conviction of a secondary party. The prosecution must prove the principal committed the AR — but the principal need not have been convicted.
Key cases:
1. R v Cogan and Leak [1976]: Cogan acquitted (genuine belief in consent); Leak still convicted — the AR of rape was established.
2. R v Pickford [1995]: secondary party may be convicted where principal found not guilty by reason of insanity.

**

**Exception:**** if the acquittal demonstrates no offence was committed at all, the secondary party cannot be convicted.

⚠️ The acquittal of the principal is evidentially relevant but not determinative.
Principal & Secondary Liability.summary_line: SECONDARY PARTY · PRINCIPAL ACQUITTED · CAN STILL CONVICT ACCESSORY · DIFFERENT DEFENCES AVAILABLE · R V BOURNE

Innocent Agency.front: What is the innocent agency doctrine?
Innocent Agency.answer:
Innocent agency arises where D uses an innocent person as an instrument to commit the AR. The innocent agent lacks MR (or capacity) and is not criminally liable. D is the principal offender.
Examples:
1. D sends a child (under 10) to shoplift — D is the principal thief
2. D deceives a pharmacist into dispensing poison — D is the principal
Significance: D cannot be charged as a secondary party (there is no guilty principal).

**

**Limits:**** cannot apply to offences requiring the principal to have a specific personal characteristic.

⚠️ Exam trap: innocent agency makes D the principal — accessory liability is not applicable where the agent is innocent.
Innocent Agency.summary_line: INNOCENT AGENCY · PRINCIPAL USES INNOCENT PERSON · D IS PRINCIPAL · CHILD OR INSANE PERSON · R V MICHAEL

Secondary Liability — Key Cases.issue: Whether D who added alcohol to a friend's drink — without their knowledge — causing the friend to exceed the drink-driving limit, could be convicted as an accessory.
Secondary Liability — Key Cases.rule: A person can 'procure' the commission of an offence without being present at the scene, without the principal's knowledge, and without any prior agreement. Procuring requires bringing about the commission of the offence by D's deliberate act.
Secondary Liability — Key Cases.application: D secretly laced a friend's soft drinks with alcohol. The friend, not knowing he was over the limit, drove home and was convicted of drink-driving. The CA held D could be convicted as an accessory for procuring — D had deliberately and successfully brought about the commission of the offence.
Secondary Liability — Key Cases.conclusion: A-G's Reference (No 1 of 1975) defines procuring as the fourth mode of secondary liability. It requires a causal link between D's act and the commission of the offence. Confirms secondary liability does not require prior agreement, presence at the scene, or the principal's knowledge.
Secondary Liability — Key Cases.summary_line: A-G'S REFERENCE NO 1 OF 1975 · PROCURING · NO AGREEMENT NEEDED · CAUSING BY POSITIVE ACT · SECONDARY LIABILITY

Mens Rea for Secondary Liability — Key C.subtopic: Mens Rea for Secondary Liability — Key Cases
Mens Rea for Secondary Liability — Key C.issue: What level of knowledge is required for secondary liability — must D know the specific details of the offence planned?
Mens Rea for Secondary Liability — Key C.rule: For secondary liability, D must know the type of crime the principal intends to commit. D need not know the specific details — the particular premises, the precise time, or the exact nature of the target.
Mens Rea for Secondary Liability — Key C.application: D supplied oxygen-cutting equipment to a person who later used it to break into a bank. D claimed he did not know it would be used for bank burglary specifically. The CA upheld his conviction: knowing the equipment would be used for some kind of breaking offence was sufficient.
Mens Rea for Secondary Liability — Key C.conclusion: Bainbridge sets the knowledge threshold for accessory liability at the level of the offence type — not the specific offence. Read alongside Johnson v Youden [1950] — D must know the essential matters, not merely suspect that something illegal might happen. Suspicion alone is insufficient.
Mens Rea for Secondary Liability — Key C.summary_line: R V BAINBRIDGE 1960 · SECONDARY LIABILITY MR · KNOWLEDGE OF TYPE OF CRIME · NOT PRECISE DETAILS · BURGLARY TOOLS

Joint Enterprise — Key Cases.issue: Whether foresight alone — without intention — was sufficient to found secondary liability in a joint enterprise for a further offence committed by the principal.
Joint Enterprise — Key Cases.rule: A secondary party in a joint enterprise is liable for a further offence committed by the principal only if they intentionally assisted or encouraged the commission of that further offence. Foresight is only evidence from which intention may be inferred — it is not itself sufficient.
Joint Enterprise — Key Cases.application: Jogee encouraged his companion Hirsi during a confrontation. Hirsi stabbed and killed V. Under the pre-Jogee law (Chan Wing-Siu), Jogee's foresight that Hirsi might use a weapon was sufficient for murder. The SC quashed his murder conviction: foresight alone was insufficient. A manslaughter conviction was substituted.
Joint Enterprise — Key Cases.conclusion: Jogee fundamentally reformed the law of joint enterprise, overruling Chan Wing-Siu v R [1985] and reversing 30 years of precedent. The threshold for secondary liability was raised from foresight to intention. The most significant criminal law decision of the decade.
Joint Enterprise — Key Cases.summary_line: R V JOGEE 2016 · JOINT ENTERPRISE · PAL ABOLISHED · INTENTION NOT FORESIGHT · SC · OVERRULED CHAN WING-SIU

Scenario Cards — Participation.front: Ben drives Carl to a shop. Carl goes in and steals goods while Ben waits outside with the engine running, as agreed. Ben says he is not guilty because he did not enter the shop. Advise Ben on his liability.
Scenario Cards — Participation.answer: Ben is liable as a secondary party (accessory) to theft. Secondary liability under s.8 Accessories and Abettors Act 1861 attaches to anyone who aids, abets, counsels, or procures an offence. Ben aided Carl by driving him to the scene and waiting to facilitate the escape — classic assistance. The mens rea requires knowledge of the essential elements of the principal's offence and an intention to assist (R v Bainbridge [1960]; Johnson v Youden [1950]). Ben knew Carl was going in to steal and intentionally provided the getaway. Physical presence at the scene of the offence is not required for secondary liability. Ben is equally liable with Carl for the theft.
Scenario Cards — Participation.summary_line: SECONDARY LIABILITY · S.8 ACCESSORIES AND ABETTORS ACT 1861 · AIDING · PRESENCE NOT REQUIRED · R V BAINBRIDGE · KNOWLEDGE AND INTENT TO ASSIST

Scenario Cards — Participation.front: Dan and Ed agree to burgle a house. During the burglary, Ed produces a knife and stabs the householder, Frank, killing him. Dan had no idea Ed had a knife and did not foresee that Ed might use lethal violence. Dan is charged with murder as a secondary party. Advise on Dan's liability post-Jogee.
Scenario Cards — Participation.answer: Post-R v Jogee [2016], Dan is not guilty of murder. The Supreme Court in Jogee overruled the parasitic accessory liability doctrine from Chan Wing-Siu [1985], which had held that foresight of the principal's possible commission of a further offence was sufficient MR for secondary liability. The correct test post-Jogee is that a secondary party must have intended to assist or encourage the principal to commit the specific offence. Dan did not intend to assist or encourage Ed to murder Frank — he did not know Ed had a knife and did not foresee lethal violence. Dan cannot be convicted of murder. However, Dan may be convicted of the burglary as the agreed common purpose. He may also be assessed for manslaughter if he foresaw that Ed might cause some harm (short of the specific intent required for murder).
Scenario Cards — Participation.summary_line: SECONDARY LIABILITY · R V JOGEE · CHAN WING-SIU OVERRULED · INTENTION TO ASSIST REQUIRED · FORESIGHT ALONE INSUFFICIENT · PARASITIC ACCESSORY LIABILITY

Scenario Cards — Participation.front: Greg and Harry plan an assault on Ivan. Moments before the attack begins, Greg says 'I want no part of this' and walks away. Harry proceeds to assault Ivan alone. Is Greg's withdrawal effective?
Scenario Cards — Participation.answer: Greg's withdrawal is almost certainly ineffective. To withdraw from secondary liability, a party must do more than simply walk away — effective withdrawal requires timely and unequivocal communication of withdrawal that is sufficient to negate any prior assistance or encouragement (R v Becerra [1976]). The requirements are more stringent the closer to the commission of the offence. On these facts, Greg's last-minute statement of 'I want no part of this' immediately before the attack is too late and too passive. He had already aided and encouraged Harry (agreeing to the plan, presumably being present). There is no evidence he took steps to undo his prior assistance (e.g. by warning Ivan or contacting police). Greg remains liable as a secondary party to the assault.
Scenario Cards — Participation.summary_line: WITHDRAWAL FROM SECONDARY LIABILITY · R V BECERRA · TIMELY AND UNEQUIVOCAL · UNDO PRIOR ASSISTANCE · LAST-MINUTE WITHDRAWAL INSUFFICIENT

Murder & Manslaughter.front: What is the actus reus and mens rea of murder?
Murder & Manslaughter.answer:
Murder: the unlawful killing of a human being under the King's Peace with malice aforethought.
AR: unlawful killing of a person in being.
MR (malice aforethought): intention to kill OR intention to cause GBH: R v Vickers [1957]; R v Moloney [1985]. Includes direct and oblique (Woollin) intention. Mandatory life imprisonment.

⚠️ Key trap: intention to cause GBH — not death — suffices for murder.
Murder & Manslaughter.summary_line: MURDER · UNLAWFUL KILLING · QUEEN'S PEACE · HUMAN BEING · MALICE AFORETHOUGHT · GBH MR SUFFICIENT · R V VICKERS

Murder & Manslaughter.front: What are the three conditions for the loss of control partial defence under the CJA 2009?
Murder & Manslaughter.answer:
Loss of control is a partial defence to murder under ss.54–55 CJA 2009:
1. D lost self-control at the time of the killing (s.54(1)(a)) — need not be sudden; must be genuine.
2. Qualifying trigger (s.55): fear trigger (fear of serious violence from V); OR circumstance trigger (circumstances of extremely grave character causing D a justifiable sense of being seriously wronged). Sexual jealousy alone expressly excluded: s.55(6)(c).
3. A person of D's sex and age with a normal degree of tolerance and self-restraint might have reacted in the same or similar way (s.54(1)(c)).

⚠️ Exam trap: if D incited the feared violence or circumstances as a pretext, the defence is unavailable: s.54(4).
Murder & Manslaughter.summary_line: LOSS OF CONTROL · S.54 CJA 2009 · THREE CONDITIONS · QUALIFYING TRIGGER · SEXUAL JEALOUSY EXCLUSION · OBJECTIVE LIMB

Murder & Manslaughter.front: What are the elements of diminished responsibility under the Coroners and Justice Act 2009?
Murder & Manslaughter.answer:
D must prove (on balance of probabilities) under s.2 HA 1957 as amended:
1. An abnormality of mental functioning
2. Arising from a recognised medical condition
3. Which substantially impaired D's ability to: (a) understand the nature of their conduct; (b) form a rational judgement; or (c) exercise self-control
4. The abnormality provides an explanation for D's acts
Voluntary intoxication alone does not constitute a recognised medical condition. But ADS may qualify: R v Wood [2008]; R v Stewart [2009].

⚠️ Exam trap: D must prove all four elements.
Murder & Manslaughter.summary_line: DIMINISHED RESPONSIBILITY · S.2 HOMICIDE ACT 1957 AS AMENDED · ABNORMALITY OF MENTAL FUNCTIONING · RECOGNISED MEDICAL CONDITION · SUBSTANTIAL IMPAIRMENT

Murder & Manslaughter.front: How does the sexual jealousy exclusion in s.55(6)(c) CJA 2009 interact with the circumstance trigger?
Murder & Manslaughter.answer:
Section 55(6)(c) excludes sexual infidelity as a qualifying trigger. However, in R v Clinton [2012], the Court of Appeal held the exclusion cannot operate to remove from the jury's consideration all circumstances simply because sexual infidelity was one element. Where sexual infidelity forms part of a wider set of circumstances (e.g. taunting remarks, D's mental state), those other circumstances should not be excluded.

⚠️ Clinton limits s.55(6)(c) — the exclusion is narrower than a complete bar where sexual jealousy is part of a wider factual matrix.
Murder & Manslaughter.summary_line: SEXUAL JEALOUSY EXCLUSION · S.55(6)(C) CJA 2009 · INCITEMENT BY SEXUAL INFIDELITY · CANNOT CONSTITUTE QUALIFYING TRIGGER · R V CLINTON

Murder & Manslaughter.front: What are the elements of unlawful act manslaughter?
Murder & Manslaughter.answer:
1. An unlawful act — must be a criminal offence: R v Franklin [1883]. Omissions do not suffice.
2. The act must be dangerous — sober and reasonable bystander would recognise risk of some harm: R v Church [1966].
3. The unlawful dangerous act must cause death.
4. D must have MR for the unlawful act — not for death or harm: DPP v Newbury and Jones [1977].
Drug supply: V's free and voluntary self-injection breaks the chain: R v Kennedy (No 2) [2007].

⚠️ Key trap: D needs MR only for the underlying unlawful act — not for the death.
Murder & Manslaughter.summary_line: UNLAWFUL ACT MANSLAUGHTER · CONSTRUCTIVE MANSLAUGHTER · UNLAWFUL ACT · DANGEROUS · R V CHURCH · SOBER BYSTANDER TEST

Murder & Manslaughter.front: What is the Adomako test for gross negligence manslaughter?
Murder & Manslaughter.answer:
R v Adomako [1995] HL:
1. D owed V a duty of care
2. D breached that duty
3. The breach caused V's death
4. The breach was so grossly negligent that it should be judged criminal by the jury
The risk must be a risk of death — not merely serious injury: R v Rudling [2016]; R v Rose [2017].

⚠️ Exam trap: the risk that creates the breach must be a risk of death — not merely serious injury.
Murder & Manslaughter.summary_line: GROSS NEGLIGENCE MANSLAUGHTER · ADOMAKO TEST · DUTY OF CARE · BREACH · RISK OF DEATH · GROSS NEGLIGENCE · JURY QUESTION

Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.subtopic: Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Diminished Responsibility
Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.front: What characteristics can be taken into account in the objective limb of the loss of control defence?
Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.answer:
The objective limb (s.54(1)(c) CJA 2009) asks whether a person of D's sex and age, with a normal degree of tolerance and self-restraint and in the circumstances of D, might have reacted in the same or similar way.
Sex and age only are explicitly included as modifiers to the standard of tolerance and self-restraint.
Other personal characteristics (depression, PTSD, short temper) are excluded from lowering the objective standard: R v Asmelash [2013].

⚠️ Exam trap: mental health conditions or personal vulnerabilities do not lower the objective standard of self-restraint.
Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.summary_line: LOSS OF CONTROL · OBJECTIVE LIMB CHARACTERISTICS · AGE · SEX · R V CLINTON · NOT ALL CHARACTERISTICS · NORMAL DEGREE OF TOLERANCE

Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.subtopic: Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Diminished Responsibility
Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.front: How does voluntary intoxication interact with the diminished responsibility defence?
Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.answer:
Voluntary intoxication alone does not constitute a 'recognised medical condition' for diminished responsibility. However, where D suffers from alcohol dependency syndrome (ADS) causing brain damage or a recognised psychiatric disorder, that underlying condition may ground the defence.
R v Wood [2008]: where D suffers from ADS, the jury must assess whether the abnormality arose from the ADS itself — not merely from acute voluntary intoxication at the time.
R v Stewart [2009]: confirmed ADS can qualify.

⚠️ Exam trap: acute voluntary intoxication = no defence; underlying recognised condition caused by chronic alcohol dependency = potential defence.
Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.summary_line: VOLUNTARY INTOXICATION · DIMINISHED RESPONSIBILITY · ALCOHOL DEPENDENCY SYNDROME · R V WOOD · R V STEWART · UNDERLYING CONDITION

Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.subtopic: Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Diminished Responsibility
Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.front: What is the contrast between loss of control and diminished responsibility as partial defences to murder?
Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.answer:
Both reduce murder to voluntary manslaughter. Key contrasts:
Loss of control: qualifying trigger required / requires actual loss of control / prosecution must disprove once raised / not available for revenge/planned killing / sexual jealousy expressly excluded
Diminished responsibility: recognised medical condition required / no loss of control required / D must prove on balance of probabilities / available even if deliberate / no sexual jealousy exclusion

⚠️ Exam trap: the burden differs — loss of control: prosecution must negative; diminished responsibility: D bears the burden.
Partial Defences — Loss of Control & Dim.summary_line: LOSS OF CONTROL VS DIMINISHED RESPONSIBILITY · SUDDEN REACTION VS ABNORMALITY · OBJECTIVE VS SUBJECTIVE · CAN BOTH APPLY · STRATEGY

Gross Negligence & Corporate Manslaughte.front: Can gross negligence manslaughter apply in non-medical contexts?
Gross Negligence & Corporate Manslaughte.answer:
Yes. The Adomako test applies wherever D owes V a duty of care.
R v Willoughby [2004]: D owned a pub and engaged a man to help burn it for insurance fraud. The man died. CA held Willoughby owed a duty as the person who created the dangerous situation and controlled the premises. Convicted of GNM.
R v Evans [2009]: drug dealer who supplied heroin then failed to summon help owed a duty arising from the dangerous situation she helped create — applying Miller principles.
The duty of care question is determined by the judge as a matter of law.

⚠️ The duty of care is a legal question — it cannot be left to the jury.
Gross Negligence & Corporate Manslaughte.summary_line: GROSS NEGLIGENCE MANSLAUGHTER · NON-MEDICAL CONTEXTS · R V ADOMAKO · R V EVANS · SUPPLY OF DRUGS · DUTY CAN ARISE

Gross Negligence & Corporate Manslaughte.front: What is corporate manslaughter under the Corporate Manslaughter and Corporate Homicide Act 2007?
Gross Negligence & Corporate Manslaughte.answer:
Corporate manslaughter under s.1 CMCHA 2007:
AR: an organisation causes a person's death; how activities are managed by senior management constitutes a gross breach of a duty of care; senior management's role is a substantial element of the breach.

**

**Key features:****
1. Only committed by an organisation — not an individual
2. Senior management = persons playing a significant role in decisions
3. Identification doctrine abandoned — focus on systemic failures
Sentence: unlimited fine; remedial order; publicity order. No custodial sentence.

⚠️ Key change: the common law requirement to identify a 'directing mind' first is removed.
Gross Negligence & Corporate Manslaughte.summary_line: CORPORATE MANSLAUGHTER · CORPORATE MANSLAUGHTER AND HOMICIDE ACT 2007 · GROSS BREACH · SENIOR MANAGEMENT · DUTY OF CARE · FINE AND PUBLICITY ORDER

Murder — Key Cases.issue: Whether intention to cause grievous bodily harm — without any intention to kill — was sufficient MR for murder.
Murder — Key Cases.rule: The MR for murder ('malice aforethought') includes not only an intention to kill but also an intention to cause grievous bodily harm.
Murder — Key Cases.application: D broke into a shop. He was disturbed by an elderly shopkeeper and hit her several times, killing her. D did not intend to kill her — but the Court of Criminal Appeal held that intention to cause GBH sufficed.
Murder — Key Cases.conclusion: Vickers establishes the implied malice limb of murder MR — an intention to cause GBH suffices. Frequently tested: D who intends serious injury but not death is guilty of murder if V dies. Confirmed by DPP v Smith [1961] and R v Moloney [1985]. The rule has been criticised as disproportionate but is firmly established.
Murder — Key Cases.summary_line: R V VICKERS 1957 · MURDER · MALICE AFORETHOUGHT · GBH MR SUFFICIENT · INTENTION TO CAUSE GBH · UNLAWFUL KILLING

Murder — Key Cases.issue: Whether foresight of the probability of death or GBH was equivalent to intention for murder.
Murder — Key Cases.rule: Intention for murder means purpose or aim. Foresight of consequences — even of a high probability — is not equivalent to intention. It is only evidence from which intention may be inferred.
Murder — Key Cases.application: D and his stepfather drunkenly challenged each other to a shooting contest. D fired and killed his stepfather. The HL held the trial judge had misdirected the jury by equating foresight of probable consequences with intention. The correct approach: did D aim to kill or cause GBH?
Murder — Key Cases.conclusion: Moloney establishes that intention is the core MR concept for murder and cannot be equated with foresight. The case laid the groundwork for Nedrick and Woollin's virtual certainty test. The core principle — intention ≠ foresight — remains foundational.
Murder — Key Cases.summary_line: R V MOLONEY 1985 · MURDER · DIRECT INTENTION · OBLIQUE INTENTION · NATURAL CONSEQUENCE · HL · NEDRICK FOLLOWED

Manslaughter — Key Cases.issue: What is the correct test for whether an act is 'dangerous' for the purposes of unlawful act manslaughter?
Manslaughter — Key Cases.rule: An unlawful act is dangerous if all sober and reasonable people who witnessed it would inevitably recognise that it subjected the victim to the risk of some harm — not necessarily serious harm. The test is objective.
Manslaughter — Key Cases.application: D attacked a woman, believing he had killed her, and threw her body into a river. She was in fact still alive and drowned. The CA held D guilty of manslaughter: his unlawful act (the attack) was dangerous because a sober and reasonable bystander would have recognised it as carrying a risk of some physical harm.
Manslaughter — Key Cases.conclusion: Church sets the 'dangerous act' standard for unlawful act manslaughter. The test is objective and relatively low — some harm is required, not serious harm. The case also applies the transaction principle. Church remains the definitive statement of the dangerous act test.
Manslaughter — Key Cases.summary_line: R V CHURCH 1966 · UNLAWFUL ACT MANSLAUGHTER · DANGEROUS ACT TEST · SOBER BYSTANDER · RECOGNISE RISK OF HARM

Manslaughter — Key Cases.issue: Whether a drug supplier was guilty of unlawful act manslaughter where the recipient freely and voluntarily self-injected and died.
Manslaughter — Key Cases.rule: Where a victim makes a free, voluntary, and informed decision to self-administer a drug supplied by D, the victim's act constitutes a novus actus interveniens breaking the chain of causation. D cannot be liable for manslaughter in these circumstances.
Manslaughter — Key Cases.application: D prepared a heroin syringe and handed it to V, who self-injected and died. The HL quashed D's manslaughter conviction: V's free and voluntary act of self-injection broke the chain of causation. D had not administered the drug — V had.
Manslaughter — Key Cases.conclusion: Kennedy (No 2) is the definitive authority on drug supply and unlawful act manslaughter. It overruled earlier CA decisions and confirmed that free and voluntary self-injection breaks causation. Liability may still arise under other routes — e.g. GNM where D had a duty to seek help: R v Evans.
Manslaughter — Key Cases.summary_line: R V KENNEDY NO 2 2007 · CAUSATION · DRUG SUPPLY · FREE AND VOLUNTARY ACT · BREAKS CHAIN · HL

Manslaughter — Key Cases.issue: What is the correct test for gross negligence manslaughter?
Manslaughter — Key Cases.rule:
Gross negligence manslaughter requires:

1. D owed V a duty of care
2. D breached that duty
3. the breach caused V's death
4. the breach was so grossly negligent that the jury regards it as criminal.
Manslaughter — Key Cases.application: D was an anaesthetist who failed to notice that a patient's breathing tube had become disconnected. The patient died. The HL held D guilty: there was a duty of care, a serious breach, causation, and the breach was sufficiently gross to be criminal.
Manslaughter — Key Cases.conclusion: Adomako abolished the Seymour/Lawrence objective recklessness approach and replaced it with the gross negligence test. The apparent circularity of the 'criminal in the jury's assessment' test was upheld as compliant with Article 7 in R v Misra [2004].
Manslaughter — Key Cases.summary_line: R V ADOMAKO 1995 · GROSS NEGLIGENCE MANSLAUGHTER · DUTY · BREACH · RISK OF DEATH · GROSSLY NEGLIGENT · HL

Partial Defences — Loss of Control — Key.subtopic: Partial Defences — Loss of Control — Key Cases
Partial Defences — Loss of Control — Key.issue: Whether the sexual infidelity exclusion in s.55(6)(c) CJA 2009 prevented the jury from considering any circumstances related to sexual infidelity as part of the qualifying trigger for loss of control.
Partial Defences — Loss of Control — Key.rule: The s.55(6)(c) exclusion prevents sexual infidelity alone from constituting the qualifying trigger. However, where sexual infidelity forms part of a wider factual picture including other grave circumstances, those other circumstances are not excluded merely because sexual infidelity was also present.
Partial Defences — Loss of Control — Key.application: D killed his wife after she confessed to multiple affairs and made taunting remarks about their children. D suffered from depression. The trial judge withdrew the loss of control defence. The CA allowed the appeal: the taunting remarks and D's mental state were separate circumstances not solely reducible to sexual infidelity.
Partial Defences — Loss of Control — Key.conclusion: Clinton limits the s.55(6)(c) exclusion to cases where the qualifying trigger is solely or purely sexual infidelity. Where other grave circumstances exist alongside sexual infidelity, those other circumstances are not contaminated by the exclusion.
Partial Defences — Loss of Control — Key.summary_line: R V CLINTON 2012 · LOSS OF CONTROL · SEXUAL INFIDELITY · CONTEXT · QUALIFYING TRIGGER · S.55(6)(C)

Partial Defences — Diminished Responsibi.subtopic: Partial Defences — Diminished Responsibility — Key Cases
Partial Defences — Diminished Responsibi.issue: Whether alcohol dependency syndrome could constitute a 'recognised medical condition' for diminished responsibility.
Partial Defences — Diminished Responsibi.rule: Alcohol dependency syndrome (ADS) can constitute a recognised medical condition. Where ADS is the underlying condition, the jury must distinguish between the effects of the ADS itself — which may ground the defence — and the effects of acute voluntary intoxication at the time.
Partial Defences — Diminished Responsibi.application: D killed V during a sustained attack. Medical evidence established severe ADS that had caused brain damage. D had also been drinking that night. The CA held the jury should have been directed to consider what impairment resulted from the ADS as a disease rather than from acute intoxication.
Partial Defences — Diminished Responsibi.conclusion: Wood (confirmed in R v Stewart [2009]) establishes that ADS can ground diminished responsibility. The key analytical step is separating the ADS-caused abnormality from the voluntary intoxication at the time.
Partial Defences — Diminished Responsibi.summary_line: R V WOOD 2008 · DIMINISHED RESPONSIBILITY · ALCOHOL DEPENDENCY SYNDROME · RECOGNISED MEDICAL CONDITION · CA

Gross Negligence Manslaughter — Key Case.issue: Whether a person who supplied heroin and then failed to summon help when V began to show signs of overdose owed a duty of care capable of grounding gross negligence manslaughter.
Gross Negligence Manslaughter — Key Case.rule: A duty of care for gross negligence manslaughter can arise from a prior dangerous act — applying the Miller principle. Where D has contributed to the creation of a dangerous situation and is aware of that danger, D owes a duty to take reasonable steps to avert it.
Gross Negligence Manslaughter — Key Case.application: D gave heroin to her 16-year-old half-sister V, who self-injected. V showed signs of overdose. D and her mother watched V overnight but did not call for help. V died. The CA held D owed a duty of care arising from her contribution to the life-threatening situation.
Gross Negligence Manslaughter — Key Case.conclusion: Evans extends the Miller prior dangerous act principle to gross negligence manslaughter. It confirms that the duty of care in GNM is not limited to pre-existing relationships — it can arise from D's own prior conduct. Read alongside Adomako and Kennedy (No 2).
Gross Negligence Manslaughter — Key Case.summary_line: R V EVANS 2009 · GROSS NEGLIGENCE MANSLAUGHTER · DUTY FROM DANGEROUS SITUATION · DRUG SUPPLY · OMISSION

Scenario Cards — Homicide.front: Jake stabs Kim once in the stomach during an argument. He claims he 'didn't mean to kill her, just to hurt her.' Kim dies. The prosecution charges murder. Does Jake have the MR for murder if he only intended serious harm?
Scenario Cards — Homicide.answer: Yes. The mens rea for murder is intention to kill or intention to cause grievous bodily harm (GBH) (R v Vickers [1957]; R v Cunningham [1981]). GBH means really serious harm (DPP v Smith [1961]). If Jake intended to cause really serious harm by stabbing Kim in the stomach, that is sufficient mens rea for murder even though he did not intend death. A single stab wound to the abdomen with a knife is strong evidence of intention to cause GBH at minimum. Jake cannot reduce the charge to manslaughter simply by denying an intent to kill — the intent to cause serious harm is independently sufficient. The jury must be directed on both limbs of the murder MR.
Scenario Cards — Homicide.summary_line: MURDER MR · INTENT TO KILL OR CAUSE GBH · R V VICKERS · R V CUNNINGHAM · DPP V SMITH · SERIOUS HARM SUFFICIENT · BOTH LIMBS

Scenario Cards — Homicide.front: Lena kills her abusive partner Mike after years of abuse. She acts when Mike is asleep. She is charged with murder. She seeks to rely on loss of control under s.54 Coroners and Justice Act 2009. Advise on the availability of the defence.
Scenario Cards — Homicide.answer:
Loss of control under ss.54-55 CJA 2009 requires:

1. D lost self-control at the time of the killing
2. there was a qualifying trigger
3. a person of D's sex and age with a normal degree of tolerance and self-restraint might have reacted similarly. The defence faces a serious difficulty here: s.54
2. requires that the loss of control was not a result of D's 'incited' reaction. More critically, if Lena acted when Mike was asleep, this strongly suggests she did not lose self-control at the moment of killing — the defence requires a sudden and temporary loss of control, though 'sudden' is not required in the same strict sense as the old provocation defence. The qualifying trigger could be s.55
4. (fear of serious violence) or s.55
5. (circumstances of an extremely grave character).

However, the planned nature of a killing while the victim sleeps makes it very difficult to establish an actual loss of control. Diminished responsibility may be a stronger alternative defence.
Scenario Cards — Homicide.summary_line: LOSS OF CONTROL · SS.54-55 CJA 2009 · QUALIFYING TRIGGER · LOSS OF CONTROL WHILE ASLEEP PROBLEMATIC · FEAR OF SERIOUS VIOLENCE · CONSIDER DIMINISHED RESPONSIBILITY

Scenario Cards — Homicide.front: Neil kills his wife after discovering she has been having an affair. He claims he lost control when he found out. He seeks to rely on loss of control. The prosecution points to s.55(6)(c) CJA 2009. Advise.
Scenario Cards — Homicide.answer: Neil's loss of control defence will fail. Section 55(6)(c) Coroners and Justice Act 2009 expressly provides that sexual infidelity cannot constitute or contribute to a qualifying trigger for the purposes of loss of control. This is an absolute statutory exclusion — even where sexual infidelity operates alongside other triggers, the court must disregard the infidelity element in assessing the trigger (R v Clinton [2012]). The Court of Appeal in Clinton confirmed that while sexual infidelity alone cannot be a qualifying trigger, it may be considered in context where other qualifying triggers exist. On these facts, the discovery of the affair appears to be the sole trigger. Neil cannot rely on loss of control. He might consider diminished responsibility if there is psychiatric evidence of an abnormality of mental functioning.
Scenario Cards — Homicide.summary_line: LOSS OF CONTROL · S.55(6)(C) CJA 2009 · SEXUAL INFIDELITY EXCLUDED · R V CLINTON · ABSOLUTE EXCLUSION · DIMINISHED RESPONSIBILITY ALTERNATIVE

Scenario Cards — Homicide.front: Dr Patel, a consultant anaesthetist, fails to check a patient's breathing during an operation. The patient stops breathing and dies. A routine check would have revealed the problem. Dr Patel is charged with gross negligence manslaughter. What must the prosecution prove?
Scenario Cards — Homicide.answer:
Gross negligence manslaughter under R v Adomako [1994] requires:

1. D owed a duty of care to the victim — clearly established here by the doctor-patient relationship
2. D was in breach of that duty — failing to monitor breathing during anaesthesia is a clear breach of the standard of care expected of a competent anaesthetist
3. the breach caused the death — but for the failure to check, the problem would have been detected and death prevented
4. the breach was so grossly negligent that it ought to be judged criminal by the jury.

The jury must assess whether the conduct fell so far below the standard of a competent practitioner that it justifies criminal sanction — not merely civil negligence. The absence of a routine check resulting in preventable death is strong evidence of the requisite gross negligence.
Scenario Cards — Homicide.summary_line: GROSS NEGLIGENCE MANSLAUGHTER · R V ADOMAKO · DUTY OF CARE · BREACH · CAUSATION · GROSS NEGLIGENCE · JURY ASSESSMENT · DOCTOR-PATIENT

Scenario Cards — Homicide.front: Rio Construction Ltd operates a large building site. A worker, Sam, falls to his death from an inadequately guarded scaffold. The site manager, Tom, was aware of the safety issue and had been told by the company's safety officer to fix it but took no action. Rio Construction is charged with corporate manslaughter. Advise on the company's liability.
Scenario Cards — Homicide.answer:
Rio Construction may be liable under s.1 Corporate Manslaughter and Corporate Homicide Act 2007. The offence requires:

1. the organisation owed a relevant duty of care — employers owe a duty to employees (s.2(1)(a))
2. the organisation's activities were managed or organised in a way that caused the worker's death
3. that management failure amounted to a gross breach of the duty
4. the gross breach involved the way in which the organisation's senior management organised or managed its activities.

The key question is whether Tom qualifies as 'senior management' — defined as persons who play a significant role in making decisions about, or managing, whole or a substantial part of, the organisation's activities. A site manager may qualify depending on Rio's structure. The awareness of the risk and failure to act despite specific warnings is strong evidence of gross breach.

⚠️ Convictions under CMCHA 2007 have been rare in practice — the senior management requirement has proved difficult to satisfy for large organisations.
Scenario Cards — Homicide.summary_line: CORPORATE MANSLAUGHTER · CMCHA 2007 S.1 · RELEVANT DUTY OF CARE · GROSS BREACH · SENIOR MANAGEMENT ROLE · EMPLOYER-EMPLOYEE DUTY · RARE IN PRACTICE

Scenario Cards — Homicide.front: Uma and Vera are fighting. Uma punches Vera. Vera, frightened, runs into the road to escape and is struck by a car and killed. Uma is charged with unlawful act manslaughter. She argues the car driver's act broke the chain of causation. Advise.
Scenario Cards — Homicide.answer: Uma is liable for unlawful act manslaughter. The assault on Vera is an unlawful act that is dangerous in the Church sense. On causation, the question is whether Vera's act of running into the road was a foreseeable response to the threat — if so, it does not break the chain. The Roberts test [1971] applies: if D's unlawful act causes V to take a reasonable evasive action that results in harm, causation is established. A victim running into the road to escape from an attacker is a foreseeable and reasonable response. The car driver's act (absent recklessness) does not break the chain — it was triggered by Uma's assault. Uma caused Vera's death for the purposes of the unlawful act manslaughter charge.
Scenario Cards — Homicide.summary_line: UNLAWFUL ACT MANSLAUGHTER · CAUSATION · ROBERTS TEST · REASONABLE EVASIVE ACTION · R V CHURCH · FORESEEABLE ESCAPE RESPONSE · CHAIN NOT BROKEN

Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.front: What is the actus reus and mens rea of assault and battery under s.39 CJA 1988?
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.answer:
Assault: an act which intentionally or recklessly causes V to apprehend immediate unlawful force. Words alone may suffice: R v Constanza [1997]; R v Ireland [1998]. MR: intention or Cunningham recklessness.
Battery: unlawful application of force to V — can be minimal. MR: intention or Cunningham recklessness as to unlawful force.
Both summary; max 6 months.

⚠️ Conditional threats can constitute assault if V apprehends immediate force. 'Immediate' means imminent — not instantaneous.
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.summary_line: ASSAULT · BATTERY · S.39 CJA 1988 · APPREHENSION OF IMMEDIATE UNLAWFUL FORCE · APPLICATION OF FORCE · R V IRELAND · R V BURSTOW

Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.front: What are the elements of ABH under s.47 OAPA 1861, and what is the mens rea trap?
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.answer:
ABH under s.47 OAPA 1861:
1. An assault (or battery)
2. Which occasions actual bodily harm (any hurt or injury to health or comfort: R v Miller [1954])
3. MR: intention or recklessness as to the assault only — not as to ABH
Psychiatric injury constituting a clinically recognisable condition can be ABH: R v Chan-Fook [1994].
Key authority: R v Roberts [1971]; R v Savage and Parmenter [1992] (HL).
Max 5 years. Either way.

⚠️ MR trap: recklessness as to the assault is sufficient even where ABH was unforeseeable to D.
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.summary_line: ABH · S.47 OAPA 1861 · ASSAULT OR BATTERY · ACTUAL BODILY HARM · R V CHAN-FOOK · MR IS SAME AS ASSAULT · PSYCHIATRIC INJURY

Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.front: What is the distinction between s.20 and s.18 GBH under the OAPA 1861?
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.answer:
s.20 — unlawful wounding or inflicting GBH: MR is intention or Cunningham recklessness as to some harm (not necessarily serious harm): R v Savage and Parmenter [1992]. Max 5 years. Either way.
s.18 — wounding or causing GBH with intent: MR is specific intention to cause GBH. Recklessness is not sufficient: R v Belfon [1976]. Max life. Indictable only.
GBH = really serious harm: DPP v Smith [1961]. Wounding = breaking of both layers of skin.

⚠️ Critical: s.20 — recklessness as to some harm; s.18 — specific intent to cause GBH.
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.summary_line: S.20 GBH · S.18 GBH · OAPA 1861 · WOUNDING OR GBH · S.20 RECKLESS · S.18 INTENTION · GRIEVOUS BODILY HARM

Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.front: What does 'wounding' mean under the OAPA 1861, and how does it differ from GBH?
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.answer:
Wounding requires breaking of both layers of skin — dermis and epidermis: Moriarty v Brooks [1834]. Internal bleeding without a break in the skin does not constitute a wound: JJC v Eisenhower [1983].
GBH means 'really serious harm': DPP v Smith [1961]. Does not require a break in the skin.

⚠️ Exam trap: a minor cut is a wound but not GBH. Serious internal injury without a break in skin is GBH but not a wound.
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.summary_line: WOUNDING · BREAK IN SKIN · BOTH LAYERS · R V EISENHOWER · NOT BRUISING · NOT INTERNAL BLEEDING · OAPA 1861

Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.front: Can psychiatric injury constitute ABH or GBH?
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.answer:
Yes — if it constitutes a clinically recognisable condition.
ABH: R v Chan-Fook [1994] — psychiatric injury is ABH if a recognised medical condition. Mere emotion is insufficient.
GBH: serious psychiatric harm can be GBH: R v Burstow [1997] — 'inflict' in s.20 does not require a battery.
R v Ireland [1997]: silent telephone calls causing recognised psychiatric harm can ground assault.

⚠️ The key: the condition must be clinically recognisable — not merely distress.
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.summary_line: PSYCHIATRIC INJURY · ABH · GBH · R V CHAN-FOOK · R V IRELAND · RECOGNISED PSYCHIATRIC CONDITION · NOT MERE DISTRESS

Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.front: What are the elements of rape under s.1 Sexual Offences Act 2003?
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.answer:
Rape under s.1 SOA 2003:
AR:
1. Penetration of vagina, anus, or mouth of B with A's penis
2. B does not consent
MR:
3. A does not reasonably believe B consents (s.1(1)(c)) — reasonableness assessed having regard to all circumstances: s.1(2)
Rape can only be committed by a male. Penetration is a continuing act: s.79(2).

⚠️ Key change from pre-2003 law: a genuine but unreasonable belief in consent does not suffice.
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.summary_line: RAPE · S.1 SOA 2003 · PENETRATION · PENIS · VAGINA ANUS MOUTH · NO CONSENT · NO REASONABLE BELIEF IN CONSENT

Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.front: What is the definition of consent under s.74 SOA 2003, and how do the evidential and conclusive presumptions operate?
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.answer:
Section 74 SOA 2003: a person consents if they agree by choice and have the freedom and capacity to make that choice.
Evidential presumptions (s.75): specified circumstances (e.g. V asleep, unconscious, subject to threats). Presumed no reasonable belief in consent — rebuttable.
Conclusive presumptions (s.76): D intentionally deceived V as to the nature or purpose of the act, or impersonated a person known personally to V. Not rebuttable.

⚠️ Exam trap: fraud as to attributes (age, wealth, HIV status) does NOT trigger s.76.
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.summary_line: CONSENT · S.74 SOA 2003 · FREEDOM AND CAPACITY · CONCLUSIVE PRESUMPTIONS · EVIDENTIAL PRESUMPTIONS · S.75 · S.76

Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.front: What are the elements of assault by penetration (s.2) and sexual assault (s.3) under the SOA 2003?
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.answer:
Assault by penetration (s.2): A penetrates vagina or anus of B with part of A's body or anything else (not a penis); penetration is sexual; B does not consent. MR: A does not reasonably believe B consents. Max: life imprisonment.
Sexual assault (s.3): A intentionally touches B; touching is sexual; B does not consent. MR: A does not reasonably believe B consents. Max: 10 years on indictment.
Touching is 'sexual' if a reasonable person would consider it so: s.78 SOA 2003.

⚠️ Assault by penetration carries the same maximum sentence as rape.
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.summary_line: ASSAULT BY PENETRATION · S.2 SOA 2003 · SEXUAL ASSAULT · S.3 SOA 2003 · TOUCHING · SEXUAL · NO CONSENT

Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.front: What is the OAPA 1861 offence hierarchy and what analytical framework applies?
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.answer:
Hierarchy (ascending seriousness):
1. Assault/battery (s.39 CJA 1988) — summary; max 6 months
2. ABH (s.47 OAPA) — either way; max 5 years
3. GBH/wounding — recklessness as to some harm (s.20) — either way; max 5 years
4. GBH/wounding with intent (s.18) — indictable only; max life

**

**Analytical framework:****

**

**Step 1:**** What harm resulted? (apprehension → assault; contact → battery; hurt to health → ABH; really serious/wound → GBH/wound)
Step 2: What was D's MR? (recklessness as to some harm → s.20; specific intent to cause GBH → s.18)
Step 3: Wound or GBH? (wound = both skin layers broken; GBH = really serious harm)

⚠️ Critical trap: s.20 and s.18 have the same AR — the distinction is entirely on MR.
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.summary_line: OAPA 1861 HIERARCHY · ANALYTICAL FRAMEWORK · S.39 · S.47 · S.20 · S.18 · ESCALATING MR · CHARGING DECISIONS

Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.front: What is the causation direction under R v Roberts, and how does it apply to ABH?
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.answer:
R v Roberts [1971]: D need not prove D intended or foresaw ABH — only that D committed an assault from which ABH resulted. The 'occasioning' element requires causation.
Roberts causation direction: where V tries to escape D's assault and is injured, D causes the ABH unless V's act was 'so daft' or unexpected that no reasonable person could have foreseen it.
R v Williams and Davis [1992]: the test is whether V's response was within the range of responses that could reasonably be anticipated.

⚠️ Exam trap: for s.47, D need only have MR for the assault. But causation requires that the ABH be a reasonably foreseeable consequence.
Assault, GBH & Sexual Offences.summary_line: CAUSATION · PHYSICAL INJURY · VICTIM'S REACTION · REASONABLY FORESEEABLE · R V ROBERTS · NOT DAFT OR UNEXPECTED

Harassment.front: What protection does the Protection from Harassment Act 1997 provide?
Harassment.answer:
Two criminal offences:
1. s.2 — harassment: a course of conduct (at least two occasions) which D knows or ought to know amounts to harassment. Summary only; max 6 months.
2. s.4 — putting a person in fear of violence: course of conduct causing another to fear violence on each occasion. Either way; max 5 years on indictment.
Civil remedy: s.3 provides an injunction backed by contempt powers.
Where harassment causes clinically recognisable psychiatric injury: may also be ABH or GBH.

⚠️ Exam trap: a single incident cannot constitute harassment — two or more occasions required.
Harassment.summary_line: HARASSMENT · PROTECTION FROM HARASSMENT ACT 1997 · COURSE OF CONDUCT · TWICE OR MORE · CIVIL AND CRIMINAL · RESTRAINING ORDER

Child Sexual Offences.front: What are the strict liability provisions in child sex offences under ss.5–13 SOA 2003?
Child Sexual Offences.answer:
Sections 5–8 SOA 2003 — offences against children under 13: strict liability as to age. No defence that D believed V was 13 or over. Absolute protection.
Sections 9–12 SOA 2003 — offences against children under 16: D has a defence if D reasonably believed V was 16 or over AND V was 13 or over: s.13.

**

**Key distinction:**** for under-13 offences, reasonable belief defence is completely unavailable. For 13–15 offences, reasonable belief in age 16+ is a defence.

⚠️ Exam trap: strict liability is absolute for the under-13 group.
Child Sexual Offences.summary_line: CHILD SEX OFFENCES · STRICT LIABILITY · UNDER 13 · S.5-8 SOA 2003 · NO CONSENT DEFENCE · UNDER 16 — REASONABLE BELIEF DEFENCE

Child Sexual Offences.front: What is the offence of sexual communication with a child under s.15A SOA 2003?
Child Sexual Offences.answer:
Section 15A SOA 2003 (inserted by s.67 Serious Crime Act 2015):
AR: A intentionally communicates with a child under 16 (B); the communication is sexual, or intended to encourage B to make a sexual communication with A.
MR: A does not reasonably believe B is 16 or over.
Mode: either way; max 2 years on indictment.
Addresses grooming behaviour at the communication stage — prior to s.15A, only meeting-based grooming (s.15) was covered.

⚠️ Exam trap: the reasonable belief defence as to age applies — contrasting with under-13 offences.
Child Sexual Offences.summary_line: SEXUAL COMMUNICATION WITH A CHILD · S.15A SOA 2003 · UNDER 16 · SEXUAL PURPOSE · ONLINE · GROOMING

Assault & ABH — Key Cases.front: R v Ireland; R v Burstow [1998]
Assault & ABH — Key Cases.issue: (Ireland) Whether silent telephone calls could constitute assault. (Burstow) Whether psychiatric injury inflicted by stalking could constitute GBH under s.20 OAPA 1861 without any physical contact.
Assault & ABH — Key Cases.rule: (Ireland) Assault can be committed by words alone — including silence. (Burstow) Psychiatric injury amounting to a clinically recognisable condition can constitute GBH. The word 'inflict' in s.20 does not require a battery or direct physical application of force.
Assault & ABH — Key Cases.application: (Ireland) D made hundreds of silent calls to three women, causing significant psychiatric illness. Held: silent calls capable of constituting assault. (Burstow) D conducted a prolonged campaign of harassment causing severe depression. Held: psychiatric harm was GBH; 'inflict' extended to psychiatric harm caused without physical contact.
Assault & ABH — Key Cases.conclusion: Ireland and Burstow together significantly expanded the scope of assault and GBH. Ireland broke the requirement for words or physical presence. Burstow confirmed GBH is not confined to physical injury. Both rulings confirmed by the HL in the same judgment. Read with Chan-Fook for ABH from psychiatric injury.
Assault & ABH — Key Cases.summary_line: R V IRELAND R V BURSTOW 1998 · ASSAULT · SILENT PHONE CALLS · PSYCHIATRIC HARM · GBH · APPREHENSION · HL

Assault & ABH — Key Cases.issue: Whether psychiatric injury — without any physical injury — could constitute 'actual bodily harm' for the purposes of s.47 OAPA 1861.
Assault & ABH — Key Cases.rule: Actual bodily harm under s.47 OAPA 1861 includes psychiatric injury, provided it constitutes a clinically recognisable medical condition. Mere emotional states — fear, distress, or panic — are insufficient.
Assault & ABH — Key Cases.application: D aggressively questioned V, locked him in a room, and made threats. V jumped from a window to escape and was injured. Medical evidence confirmed V had suffered a hysterical and nervous condition. The CA held that psychiatric injury could be ABH — provided it was a recognised clinical condition.
Assault & ABH — Key Cases.conclusion: Chan-Fook defines the scope of ABH to include psychiatric harm meeting a clinical threshold. Confirmed and extended in R v Ireland; R v Burstow [1998]. The clinically recognisable condition test is the dividing line in all psychiatric harm cases.
Assault & ABH — Key Cases.summary_line: R V CHAN-FOOK 1994 · ABH · PSYCHIATRIC INJURY · RECOGNISED CONDITION · NOT MERE EMOTIONS · BODILY HARM

GBH — Key Cases.front: R v Savage; DPP v Parmenter [1992]
GBH — Key Cases.issue: What MR is required for ABH under s.47 and for s.20 GBH — must D foresee the level of harm that results?
GBH — Key Cases.rule: For s.47 ABH: D need only have MR for the assault or battery — intention or recklessness as to causing the victim to apprehend unlawful force. D need not foresee ABH or any harm. For s.20: D must intend or be reckless as to infliction of some harm — but not serious harm.
GBH — Key Cases.application: (Savage) D threw beer over a woman; the glass slipped and cut V's wrist. HL held MR for battery sufficed — D need not foresee ABH. (Parmenter) D injured his baby son through rough handling. HL held s.47 required MR only for the assault/battery; for s.20, Cunningham recklessness as to some harm was required.
GBH — Key Cases.conclusion: Savage and Parmenter definitively settled the MR for ss.47 and 20 OAPA 1861. These offences impose constructive liability for the harm that results — MR for the underlying assault or battery (s.47), or for some harm (s.20). One of the most heavily examined distinctions in non-fatal offences.
GBH — Key Cases.summary_line: R V SAVAGE DPP V PARMENTER 1992 · S.47 MR · SAME AS ASSAULT · RECKLESSNESS AS TO TOUCHING · NOT GBH · HL

GBH — Key Cases.issue: What does 'grievous bodily harm' mean for the purposes of the OAPA 1861?
GBH — Key Cases.rule: Grievous bodily harm means really serious harm. It does not require permanent or life-threatening injury.
GBH — Key Cases.application: D drove a car erratically to dislodge a police officer who had grabbed the car. The officer fell off and was killed. The HL confirmed that GBH means 'really serious bodily harm' — at the upper end of the seriousness spectrum.
GBH — Key Cases.conclusion: DPP v Smith provides the authoritative definition of GBH. It applies to both s.18 and s.20 OAPA 1861. 'Really serious' is a jury question on the particular facts. Read with the wounding definition (Moriarty v Brooks) to understand the two alternative AR elements under both ss.18 and 20.
GBH — Key Cases.summary_line: DPP V SMITH 1961 · GBH · REALLY SERIOUS HARM · OBJECTIVE TEST FOR GRAVITY · S.18 AND S.20 · HL

Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.front: Will shouts 'I'm going to smash your face in' at Xander from across the street. Xander is frightened but Will makes no physical contact. Is Will liable for assault?
Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.answer: Yes. Common assault (assault in the narrow sense) is committed when D intentionally or recklessly causes V to apprehend immediate unlawful force (R v Ireland [1997]; R v Burstow [1997]). Words alone can constitute an assault — Ireland confirmed that a recognisable psychiatric illness caused by telephone calls could suffice. Here, Will's threat causes Xander to apprehend immediate unlawful force, satisfying the actus reus. The mens rea is intention or recklessness as to causing the apprehension. The proximity across the street goes to whether the apprehension of immediate force was reasonable — it may still be immediate even if Will is not immediately adjacent. Will is likely guilty of common assault.
Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.summary_line: ASSAULT · APPREHENSION OF IMMEDIATE FORCE · R V IRELAND · WORDS SUFFICIENT · RECKLESSNESS OR INTENTION · IMMINENCE

Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.front: Yasmin hits Zoe with a cricket bat during an argument, breaking her arm. Yasmin is charged. The prosecution must choose between s.20 and s.18 OAPA 1861. What is the critical distinction, and what additional evidence would be needed to pursue s.18?
Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.answer: The critical distinction is mens rea. Both offences require the actus reus of wounding or causing/inflicting GBH — a broken arm is a wound (broken skin) or GBH (serious harm). Section 20 requires intention or subjective recklessness as to some bodily harm (R v Savage; R v Parmenter [1992]) — this is a less onerous MR. Section 18 requires specific intention to cause GBH or to resist arrest — this is the aggravated version carrying up to life imprisonment. To pursue s.18, the prosecution would need evidence that Yasmin specifically intended to cause really serious harm: e.g., repeated blows, a targeted strike, statements before or after, or other conduct demonstrating her specific purpose. Hitting someone once with a cricket bat in an argument may support inference of GBH intent, but the jury must be directed on the specific intent requirement for s.18.
Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.summary_line: S.18 VS S.20 OAPA 1861 · MENS REA DISTINCTION · S.20 RECKLESSNESS SUFFICIENT · S.18 SPECIFIC INTENT TO CAUSE GBH · SAVAGE AND PARMENTER · LIFE IMPRISONMENT

Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.front: Aaron grabs Bella by the arm in the street, leaving a bruise. He is charged with assault occasioning actual bodily harm under s.47 OAPA 1861. What must the prosecution prove, and does Aaron need to have intended the bodily harm?
Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.answer:
For s.47 ABH the prosecution must prove:

1. an assault or battery — the unlawful grabbing is a battery
2. occasioning (causing) actual bodily harm — a bruise satisfies this, being more than transient or trifling (R v Chan-Fook [1994])
3. the required mens rea.

The critical point on MR: s.47 does not require intention or recklessness as to the ABH itself. The prosecution need only prove the mens rea for the underlying assault or battery (intention or recklessness as to the application of unlawful force) — the fact that ABH results is sufficient without any additional MR as to the level of harm (R v Roberts [1971]; R v Savage [1991]). Aaron does not need to have foreseen or intended the bruise — the battery suffices.
Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.summary_line: S.47 ABH · OAPA 1861 · ASSAULT OR BATTERY · ACTUAL BODILY HARM · CHAN-FOOK · MR FOR BATTERY ONLY · NO MR REQUIRED AS TO ABH · R V ROBERTS

Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.front: Callum makes repeated unwanted phone calls and sends dozens of messages to his ex-partner Dana over three months, causing her serious anxiety. He is charged under the Protection from Harassment Act 1997. What must the prosecution establish?
Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.answer:
Under s.1 Protection from Harassment Act 1997, the prosecution must prove:

1. a course of conduct — defined as conduct on at least two occasions (s.7(3)); three months of repeated calls and messages clearly satisfies this
2. which D knows, or ought to know, amounts to harassment — an objective test: would a reasonable person in possession of the same information consider the conduct harassment? Repeated unwanted calls and messages to an ex-partner plainly do
3. actual harassment of the victim — Dana's serious anxiety satisfies this.

The s.2 offence is the basic harassment offence. If Dana feared violence would be used against her, the more serious s.4 offence (putting people in fear of violence) may also apply. Callum's course of conduct satisfies the PHA 1997.
Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.summary_line: HARASSMENT · PHA 1997 S.1 · COURSE OF CONDUCT · TWO OR MORE OCCASIONS · OUGHT TO KNOW TEST · SERIOUS ANXIETY · S.4 IF FEAR OF VIOLENCE

Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.front: Eve, a 30-year-old, has sexual intercourse with Fred, who is 15. Eve claims Fred consented and that he told her he was 18. She did not check his age. Eve is charged with a child sexual offence. Does Fred's apparent consent or Eve's mistaken belief in his age provide a defence?
Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.answer: No. Sexual offences against children under 16 under the Sexual Offences Act 2003 are strict liability as to age in the relevant provisions. Under s.9 SOA 2003 (sexual activity with a child), the offence is committed where the victim is under 16. A mistaken belief in the child's age is not a defence unless it was a reasonable belief (s.9(1)(c): D does not reasonably believe V is 16 or over). The reasonableness of any belief in age is assessed in all the circumstances including any steps D took to ascertain V's age — Eve took no steps, only relying on Fred's assertion. A jury would likely find no reasonable belief in age. Fred's apparent consent is irrelevant — a child under 16 cannot legally consent to sexual activity for the purposes of the SOA 2003. Eve is liable under s.9.
Scenario Cards — Non-Fatal Offences.summary_line: SEXUAL ACTIVITY WITH CHILD · SOA 2003 S.9 · UNDER 16 · STRICT LIABILITY AS TO AGE · REASONABLE BELIEF DEFENCE · NO STEPS TAKEN · CONSENT IRRELEVANT

Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.front: What are the five elements of theft under ss.1–6 Theft Act 1968?
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.answer:
Theft: dishonestly appropriating property belonging to another with the intention of permanently depriving the other of it: s.1 TA 1968.
1. Appropriation (s.3): assuming any of the rights of an owner — includes consensual transactions: R v Gomez [1993]; R v Hinks [2000].
2. Property (s.4): money, real/personal property, things in action. Excludes land (mostly), confidential information: Oxford v Moss [1979].
3. Belonging to another (s.5): possession, control, or proprietary right/interest.
4. Dishonesty: s.2 negative definition + Ivey v Genting [2017] objective test.
5. ITPD (s.6): includes treating property as D's own regardless of owner's rights.
Max 7 years. Either way.
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.summary_line: THEFT · S.1 THEFT ACT 1968 · FIVE ELEMENTS · APPROPRIATION · PROPERTY · BELONGING TO ANOTHER · DISHONESTY · INTENTION TO PERMANENTLY DEPRIVE

Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.front: What is the test for dishonesty following Ivey v Genting Casinos [2017]?
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.answer:
The Supreme Court replaced the two-stage Ghosh test:
1. What was D's actual subjective state of knowledge and belief as to the facts?
2. Was D's conduct dishonest by the objective standards of ordinary decent people, assessed against the facts as D believed them?
The second Ghosh limb — did D realise their conduct was dishonest? — is removed. Confirmed as binding in Barton v Booth [2020] CA.

⚠️ Key change: D can no longer claim subjective belief that their conduct was not dishonest.
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.summary_line: DISHONESTY · IVEY V GENTING CASINOS 2017 · TWO STAGE TEST · ACTUAL KNOWLEDGE AND BELIEFS · THEN OBJECTIVE STANDARD · GHOSH OVERRULED

Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.front: What are the elements of robbery under s.8 Theft Act 1968?
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.answer:
Robbery: theft where, immediately before or at the time of stealing, D uses force on any person or puts or seeks to put any person in fear of being then and there subjected to force.
Key elements:
1. Theft must be complete
2. Force or threat of force on a person — need not be serious: R v Dawson and James [1976]
3. Immediately before or at the time of stealing
4. Force used in order to steal
Max: life imprisonment. Indictable only.

⚠️ Two traps:

1. force can be directed at any person
2. force after theft is complete = no robbery.
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.summary_line: ROBBERY · S.8 THEFT ACT 1968 · THEFT · FORCE OR THREAT · IMMEDIATELY BEFORE OR AT TIME · ON THE PERSON

Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.front: What is the distinction between s.9(1)(a) and s.9(1)(b) burglary?
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.answer:
s.9(1)(a): D enters a building or part as a trespasser with intent (at time of entry) to steal, inflict GBH, criminal damage, or rape. Ulterior offence need not be committed. MR must exist at time of entry.
s.9(1)(b): D having entered as a trespasser, steals/attempts to steal, or inflicts/attempts to inflict GBH. Offence must be committed/attempted. Intent may form after entry.
Max: 14 years (dwelling); 10 years (non-dwelling). Either way.

⚠️ Entry with permission that is exceeded becomes trespass: R v Jones and Smith [1976].
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.summary_line: BURGLARY · S.9(1)(A) · S.9(1)(B) · THEFT ACT 1968 · ENTERS BUILDING · AS TRESPASSER · MR AT TIME OF ENTRY VS AT TIME OF OFFENCE

Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.front: What are the elements of fraud by false representation under s.2 Fraud Act 2006?
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.answer:
Fraud by false representation under s.2 FA 2006:
1. D makes a representation — any express or implied statement of fact, law, or state of mind
2. The representation is false — untrue or misleading; D knows it is or might be
3. D acts dishonestly (Ivey test)
4. D intends to make a gain or cause a loss
No requirement that anyone is deceived or that gain/loss results. A machine can be the recipient: s.2(5).
Max 10 years. Either way.

⚠️ Completed on making the false representation with intent — actual deception or gain/loss is irrelevant to the AR.
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.summary_line: FRAUD BY FALSE REPRESENTATION · S.2 FRAUD ACT 2006 · FALSE · KNOWN TO BE · UNTRUE OR MISLEADING · DISHONEST · GAIN OR LOSS

Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.front: What are the elements of basic criminal damage under s.1(1) Criminal Damage Act 1971?
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.answer:
1. Destroys or damages
2. Property
3. Belonging to another
4. Without lawful excuse
5. MR: intention or Cunningham recklessness as to destroying or damaging property belonging to another
Damage is broad — temporary impairment suffices: Hardman v Chief Constable of Avon [1986].
Sentence: under £5,000 — summary only; over £5,000 — either way, max 10 years.

⚠️ Exam trap: co-owners can damage jointly owned property: R v Denton [1982].
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.summary_line: BASIC CRIMINAL DAMAGE · S.1(1) CDA 1971 · DESTROY OR DAMAGE · PROPERTY · BELONGING TO ANOTHER · INTENTION OR RECKLESSNESS

Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.front: What are the elements of aggravated criminal damage under s.1(2) CDA 1971?
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.answer:
1. Destroying or damaging property — D's own or another's
2. Intending to endanger the life of another, or being reckless as to whether life is endangered
The danger must come from the damage itself, not the manner of causing it: R v Steer [1987].
Max: life imprisonment. Indictable only.

⚠️ s.5 lawful excuse defences are NOT available to s.1(2).
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.summary_line: AGGRAVATED CRIMINAL DAMAGE · S.1(2) CDA 1971 · ENDANGERING LIFE · INTENTION OR RECKLESSNESS AS TO LIFE · R V STEER

Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.front: What are the s.5 CDA 1971 lawful excuse defences, and to which offences do they apply?
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.answer:
Section 5 defences — available to s.1

1. basic criminal damage and s.1
3. arson ONLY, NOT to s.1(2):
1. s.5(2)(a): D believed the person entitled to consent had consented or would have — belief must be genuine: R v Jaggard v Dickinson [1981].
2. s.5(2)(b): D damaged property to protect other property and believed (i) that property was in immediate need of protection; (ii) means were reasonable — both judged subjectively.

⚠️ Critical: s.5 defences do NOT apply to s.1(2).
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.summary_line: LAWFUL EXCUSE · S.5 CDA 1971 · S.5(2)(A) CONSENT · S.5(2)(B) PROTECTION OF PROPERTY · ONLY S.1(1) NOT S.1(2)

Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.front: What is the 'belonging to another' element of theft (s.5 TA 1968), and when can D steal their own property?
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.answer:
Section 5(1): property 'belongs to' any person having possession or control of it, or any proprietary right or interest.
D can appropriate their own property in two situations:
1. s.5(3) — property received under an obligation to deal with it in a particular way: R v Davidge v Bunnett [1984].
2. s.5(4) — property obtained by another's mistake where D is under an obligation to restore: R v A-G's Reference (No 1 of 1983) [1985].

⚠️ Exam trap: a person can steal their own property in law. Legal ownership does not prevent appropriation where property 'belongs to another' under s.5's broad definition.
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.summary_line: BELONGING TO ANOTHER · S.5 THEFT ACT 1968 · POSSESSION OR CONTROL · PROPRIETARY RIGHT · S.5(3) OBLIGATION TO DEAL · S.5(4) MISTAKE

Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.front: What is intention to permanently deprive under s.6 TA 1968, and when does borrowing constitute theft?
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.answer:
ITPD under s.6 TA 1968 extends to treating property as D's own regardless of the other's rights, or borrowing equivalent to outright taking.
Key applications:
1. Borrowing: only ITPD where borrowing is for a period or in circumstances equivalent to outright taking — e.g. returning a season ticket when its value has expired: R v Lloyd [1985].
2. Ransom: taking property with intent to return only on payment of ransom = ITPD.

⚠️ Exam trap: merely borrowing and returning property does not amount to theft — only if the return is after the property has lost all practical value.
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.summary_line: INTENTION TO PERMANENTLY DEPRIVE · S.6 THEFT ACT 1968 · TREAT AS OWN · BORROWING EQUIVALENT TO OUTRIGHT TAKING · R V LLOYD

Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.front: What is arson under s.1(3) CDA 1971?
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.answer:
Arson: committing criminal damage under s.1

1. or s.1
2. by fire.
Basic arson: s.1
1. committed by fire. Max: life imprisonment. Either way.
Aggravated arson: s.1
2. committed by fire — additionally requires intention or recklessness as to endangering life.

Max: life. Indictable only.
Lawful excuse: s.5 defences available to basic arson. NOT available to aggravated arson.

⚠️ Exam trap: basic arson carries life imprisonment — same maximum as aggravated arson.
Theft, Fraud & Criminal Damage.summary_line: ARSON · S.1(3) CDA 1971 · CRIMINAL DAMAGE BY FIRE · BASIC OR AGGRAVATED · LIFE ENDANGERMENT

Theft — Key Cases.issue: Whether appropriation under s.3 Theft Act 1968 could occur where V consented to the transaction.
Theft — Key Cases.rule: Appropriation under s.3 TA 1968 occurs whenever D assumes any of the rights of an owner. Consent by the owner does not prevent there being an appropriation.
Theft — Key Cases.application: D, a shop assistant, deceived his manager into accepting worthless cheques in exchange for electrical goods. The manager authorised the transaction. The HL held that the consent of the owner to the transfer did not prevent appropriation.
