Theft — Key Cases.conclusion: Gomez settled a long-running dispute about the relationship between appropriation and consent. Confirmed and extended in R v Hinks [2000]. The dishonesty element now does the work of filtering out innocent transactions.
Theft — Key Cases.summary_line: R V GOMEZ 1993 · APPROPRIATION · AUTHORISED BY OWNER · STILL AN APPROPRIATION · THEFT · HL

Theft — Key Cases.issue: Whether a person who received a valid gift from a mentally vulnerable donor could be guilty of theft.
Theft — Key Cases.rule: Appropriation can occur even where the owner makes a valid gift to D and there is no defect in the civil law transaction. D may be guilty of theft even where they receive property through a valid legal transaction, provided dishonesty is made out.
Theft — Key Cases.application: D befriended an elderly man of limited intelligence and persuaded him to give her large sums of money. The civil law gift was valid. The HL upheld D's theft conviction: there was an appropriation, the money belonged to V, and D was dishonest. Civil law validity did not negate the criminal appropriation.
Theft — Key Cases.conclusion: Hinks is the high-water mark of appropriation's broad scope. Accepting a valid gift can be theft if D is dishonest. Has attracted academic criticism for creating tension between civil and criminal law. The dishonesty element provides the necessary filter.
Theft — Key Cases.summary_line: R V HINKS 2000 · APPROPRIATION · VALID GIFT · STILL AN APPROPRIATION · CIVIL LAW IRRELEVANT · HL

Theft — Key Cases.issue: Whether the Ghosh two-stage test for dishonesty — which asked whether D realised their conduct was dishonest by ordinary standards — correctly stated the law.
Theft — Key Cases.rule:
Dishonesty:

1. ascertain D's actual subjective state of knowledge and belief as to the facts
2. ask whether D's conduct was dishonest by the objective standards of ordinary decent people, assessed against the facts as D believed them.

The second limb of Ghosh is overruled.
Theft — Key Cases.application: D, a professional card player, used 'edge sorting' to gain an advantage in baccarat, winning approximately £7.7 million. The SC held D was dishonest — his conduct was dishonest by the standards of ordinary people and D knew all the relevant facts. The second Ghosh limb was overruled.
Theft — Key Cases.conclusion: Ivey reformulates the dishonesty test by removing the subjective second limb of Ghosh. The test is now purely objective on the second question. Although technically obiter (civil case), Ivey has been followed as binding in criminal cases: Barton v Booth [2020].
Theft — Key Cases.summary_line: IVEY V GENTING CASINOS 2017 · DISHONESTY · TWO STAGE TEST · GHOSH OVERRULED · OBJECTIVE STANDARD · SC

Theft — Key Cases.issue: Whether borrowing a film reel to copy it and returning it intact constituted theft.
Theft — Key Cases.rule: Borrowing can only constitute theft where it amounts to treating the property as D's own regardless of the other's rights — specifically where D returns the property only after it has lost all its value or practical utility.
Theft — Key Cases.application: D, a cinema projectionist, removed film reels to make pirate copies and returned them before their absence was noticed. The CA held there was no ITPD — the films were returned in the same condition with all their practical value intact.
Theft — Key Cases.conclusion: Lloyd defines the scope of ITPD and confirms that mere temporary borrowing is not theft. Only where D intends to return the property in a fundamentally altered state — having extracted its practical value — does borrowing constitute ITPD. Contrast with R v Velumyl [1989].
Theft — Key Cases.summary_line: R V LLOYD 1985 · INTENTION TO PERMANENTLY DEPRIVE · BORROWING · GOODNESS AND VIRTUE GONE · S.6 TA 1968

Scenario Cards — Property Offences.front: Gary takes a bicycle from outside a shop. He intends to ride it home and leave it outside his neighbour's house for them to collect the next day, then return it to the shop. He is charged with theft. Is there an appropriation and the required intention to permanently deprive?
Scenario Cards — Property Offences.answer: There is an appropriation — any assumption of the rights of an owner suffices (s.3(1) Theft Act 1968; R v Gomez [1993]). The critical issue is intention to permanently deprive under s.1(1) TA 1968. Gary intends to return the bicycle — that negates permanent deprivation in the straightforward sense. However, s.6(1) TA 1968 extends the concept: an intention to treat the thing as one's own to dispose of regardless of the other's rights is sufficient. Borrowing for a period equivalent to an outright taking (e.g. borrowing a season ticket for the entire season) can satisfy s.6. Here, Gary plans to return the bicycle the next day in its original condition — this is unlikely to be treated as an outright taking under s.6. Gary probably lacks the necessary intent to permanently deprive and would not be guilty of theft.
Scenario Cards — Property Offences.summary_line: THEFT · TA 1968 S.1 · APPROPRIATION · S.3 GOMEZ · INTENTION TO PERMANENTLY DEPRIVE · S.6 EXTENSION · BORROWING · RETURN INTENDED

Scenario Cards — Property Offences.front: Helen approaches Ivan in the street and says 'Give me your wallet or I'll hurt you.' Ivan hands over the wallet. Helen is charged with robbery. What additional element beyond theft must the prosecution prove for robbery?
Scenario Cards — Property Offences.answer:
Robbery under s.8 Theft Act 1968 requires theft plus the use or threat of force on any person immediately before or at the time of stealing, in order to steal. The prosecution must prove:

1. theft — appropriation of the wallet belonging to another with intent to permanently deprive and dishonesty
2. force or threat of force — 'I'll hurt you' is a threat of force
3. the threat was directed at a person — satisfied
4. it was immediately before or at the time of stealing — the threat precedes the taking
5. the force/threat was used in order to steal — the threat caused Ivan to hand over the wallet.

All elements are satisfied. Helen is guilty of robbery.

**

**Note:**** even minimal force or a threat of minimal force suffices — R v Dawson and James [1976].
Scenario Cards — Property Offences.summary_line: ROBBERY · TA 1968 S.8 · THEFT PLUS FORCE OR THREAT · IMMEDIATELY BEFORE OR AT TIME · IN ORDER TO STEAL · R V DAWSON AND JAMES · MINIMAL FORCE SUFFICIENT

Scenario Cards — Property Offences.front: Jack enters Karen's house through an unlocked back door. He is looking for cash but finds none. He leaves without taking anything. Is Jack guilty of burglary?
Scenario Cards — Property Offences.answer:
Yes, Jack is guilty of burglary under s.9(1)(a) Theft Act 1968. This provision is complete at the point of entry — it does not require that the ulterior offence (theft) is actually committed. The elements are:

1. entry — Jack entered through the back door
2. as a trespasser — Karen did not consent to his entry and he had no permission
3. with intent to commit one of the specified offences — he intended to steal.

All three elements are satisfied at the point of entry. Jack is guilty of s.9(1)(a) burglary even though he found nothing to take and left empty-handed. The s.9(1)(b) alternative (committing one of the offences having entered as a trespasser) is not engaged here as no ulterior offence was committed, but s.9(1)(a) is complete.
Scenario Cards — Property Offences.summary_line: BURGLARY · TA 1968 S.9(1)(A) · ENTRY AS TRESPASSER WITH INTENT · COMPLETE AT POINT OF ENTRY · NO NEED TO COMMIT ULTERIOR OFFENCE · S.9(1)(B) DISTINGUISHED

Scenario Cards — Property Offences.front: Laura makes a false representation to a bank, claiming she is a high-net-worth individual, to obtain a £50,000 loan. She uses the money for personal expenses and makes no repayments. She is charged with fraud by false representation. Advise.
Scenario Cards — Property Offences.answer:
Laura is guilty of fraud by false representation under s.2 Fraud Act 2006. The elements are:

1. a false representation — claiming to be a high-net-worth individual when she is not is an express false statement about a matter of fact
2. D knows it is or might be false — Laura knows her representation is untrue
3. made dishonestly — applying the Ivey v Genting Casinos [2017] test: (a) what was D's actual knowledge and belief? Laura knew the truth; (b) was that dishonest by the standards of ordinary decent people? Clearly yes
4. with intent to make a gain or cause a loss — obtaining the loan is a gain.

The offence is complete on making the representation — it is not necessary that the bank actually suffers loss. Laura is guilty under s.2 Fraud Act 2006.
Scenario Cards — Property Offences.summary_line: FRAUD BY FALSE REPRESENTATION · FRAUD ACT 2006 S.2 · FALSE STATEMENT · KNOWLEDGE OF FALSITY · DISHONESTY · IVEY V GENTING · INTENT TO GAIN · COMPLETE ON REPRESENTATION

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: How are criminal offences classified, and what is the significance of classification?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
1. Summary offences: magistrates' court only (e.g. common assault, most road traffic).
2. Either-way offences: magistrates' or Crown Court (e.g. theft, ABH, s.20 GBH). Allocation procedure determines venue.
3. Indictable-only offences: Crown Court only (e.g. murder, rape, robbery, s.18 GBH). Sent under s.51 CDA 1998.
Magistrates' max: 6 months (single either-way); 12 months (two or more).

⚠️ Exam trap: D has no right to elect jury trial for summary offences.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: CLASSIFICATION OF OFFENCES · SUMMARY · EITHER-WAY · INDICTABLE ONLY · MAGISTRATES · CROWN COURT · JURISDICTION

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: What is the allocation procedure for either-way offences?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
1. Magistrates consider representations and allocation guidelines
2. Magistrates decide suitability for summary trial
3. If suitable: D given choice — consent to summary trial OR elect Crown Court
4. If D consents: proceeds in magistrates'
5. If D elects: sent to Crown Court under s.51 CDA 1998
Post-conviction: magistrates can commit to Crown Court for sentence if powers insufficient. D must be warned before consenting.

⚠️ Exam trap: D can elect Crown Court only for either-way offences.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: ALLOCATION · EITHER-WAY OFFENCES · PLEA BEFORE VENUE · GUILTY PLEA — MAGISTRATES SENTENCE · NOT GUILTY — MODE OF TRIAL · DEFENDANT ELECTION

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: What is the presumption in favour of bail under s.4 Bail Act 1976?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Section 4 BA 1976 creates a general right to bail. The presumption shifts the burden onto the prosecution to establish a ground for refusing bail.
The presumption is reduced (not eliminated) where D is charged with murder, attempted murder, manslaughter, rape, or attempted rape AND has a previous conviction for such an offence — bail must be refused unless there are exceptional circumstances: Sch.1 Part I para 6A BA 1976.

⚠️ Exam trap: it is a presumption, not an absolute right.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: BAIL · S.4 BAIL ACT 1976 · PRESUMPTION IN FAVOUR · IMPRISONABLE OFFENCES · NON-IMPRISONABLE · EXCEPTIONS

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: What are the main grounds for withholding bail for an imprisonable offence?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Bail may be withheld if there are substantial grounds for believing D would:
1. Fail to surrender to custody
2. Commit an offence while on bail
3. Interfere with witnesses or obstruct justice
Additional grounds:
4. Custody for D's own protection
5. D is already serving a custodial sentence
6. Insufficient information
7. D arrested for breach of bail conditions
8. Offence committed while D was already on bail
Factors: nature of offence; character and antecedents; record for answering bail; community ties.

⚠️ 'Substantial grounds' — not mere possibility.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: GROUNDS TO WITHHOLD BAIL · SCHEDULE 1 BAIL ACT 1976 · ABSCOND · COMMIT OFFENCE · INTERFERE WITH WITNESSES · IMPRISONABLE OFFENCES

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: What is the prosecution's duty of initial disclosure under the CPIA 1996?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Under s.3 CPIA 1996, the prosecutor must disclose any prosecution material not already disclosed that might reasonably be considered capable of undermining the prosecution's case or assisting the defence.
Prosecutor must also provide a schedule of unused material (MG6).
Continuing duty: s.7A CPIA 1996 — if new material becomes disclosable, D must be informed. Duty continues throughout proceedings.

⚠️ Exam trap: the duty is ongoing — it does not end with initial disclosure.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: INITIAL DISCLOSURE · PROSECUTION · CPIA 1996 · MATERIAL THAT MIGHT REASONABLY ASSIST DEFENCE · UNUSED MATERIAL

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: When must a defence statement be served, and what are the consequences of failure?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Compulsory in Crown Court (s.5 CPIA 1996); voluntary in magistrates' (s.6). Timing: 28 days of initial disclosure (Crown Court); 14 days (magistrates').
Contents (s.6A): nature of defence; matters of fact in issue; points of law; alibi witnesses.
Consequences of failure or inconsistency:
1. Court may draw adverse inferences: s.11 CPIA 1996
2. Prosecution or co-defendant may comment to jury

⚠️ Two-stage trap: failure doesn't prohibit running the defence — but attracts adverse inference; also triggers second round of prosecution disclosure.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: DEFENCE STATEMENT · S.5 CPIA 1996 · CROWN COURT MANDATORY · MAGISTRATES VOLUNTARY · NATURE OF DEFENCE · ALIBI · CONSEQUENCES OF NON-SERVICE

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: Under what circumstances must a confession be excluded under s.76 PACE 1984?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
A confession must be excluded under s.76(2) if obtained:
(a) By oppression: torture, inhuman or degrading treatment, or threat or use of violence. Carries its dictionary meaning: R v Fulling [1987].
(b) In consequence of anything said or done likely to render unreliable any confession — objective test.
Burden: prosecution must prove beyond reasonable doubt. Mandatory exclusion — no discretion.

⚠️ Contrast with s.78: s.76 = mandatory exclusion on specified grounds; s.78 = discretionary exclusion for general unfairness.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: EXCLUSION OF CONFESSIONS · S.76 PACE 1984 · OPPRESSION · UNRELIABLE CIRCUMSTANCES · MANDATORY EXCLUSION · PROSECUTION MUST PROVE BEYOND DOUBT

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: When may evidence be excluded under s.78 PACE 1984?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Section 78 PACE 1984 gives the court a discretion to refuse to allow evidence if, having regard to all the circumstances including how the evidence was obtained, admission would have such an adverse effect on the fairness of the proceedings that the court ought not to admit it.

**

**Key features:****
1. Discretion — not mandatory
2. Focus is on fairness of proceedings
3. Significant PACE breaches likely to lead to exclusion
4. Applied to: confessions; identification evidence; evidence obtained by deception: R v Mason [1988]

⚠️ s.78 is not a disciplinary sanction — it is a safeguard for trial fairness.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: S.78 PACE 1984 · DISCRETIONARY EXCLUSION · ADVERSE EFFECT ON FAIRNESS · BREACH OF CODES OF PRACTICE · R V MASON

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: What are the seven gateways for admissibility of a defendant's bad character under s.101 CJA 2003?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Bad character is admissible only through one of seven gateways under s.101(1) CJA 2003:
(a) All parties agree
(b) D adduces it themselves
(c) Relevant to an important matter between D and co-defendant
(d) Relevant to an important matter between D and prosecution — including propensity to commit offences or be untruthful: R v Hanson [2005]
(e) Substantial probative value in relation to a matter between D and co-defendant
(f) To correct a false impression given by D
(g) D has attacked another person's character
Gateways (d) and (g) most frequently litigated. Under (d) court may exclude if admission would adversely affect fairness: s.101(3).

⚠️ Exam trap: bad character evidence relates to the defendant — not prosecution witnesses.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: BAD CHARACTER · S.101 CJA 2003 · SEVEN GATEWAYS · DEFENDANT BAD CHARACTER · AGREEMENT · IMPORTANT EXPLANATORY EVIDENCE · PROPENSITY

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: What are the five purposes of sentencing under s.57 Sentencing Act 2020?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Section 57 Sentencing Act 2020 identifies five purposes for adult offenders:
1. Punishment of offenders
2. Reduction of crime (including by deterrence)
3. Reform and rehabilitation of offenders
4. Protection of the public
5. Making of reparation by offenders to persons affected
The court must have regard to these purposes when sentencing an offender aged 18 or over. They do not apply where the sentence is fixed by law (e.g. mandatory life for murder).
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: SENTENCING PURPOSES · S.57 SENTENCING ACT 2020 · PUNISHMENT · REDUCTION IN CRIME · REFORM · PROTECTION · REPARATION

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: What is the guilty plea discount, and what discount applies at each stage?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Sentencing Council Guideline on Reduction for Guilty Pleas:

- First stage (first hearing): one-third

- After first stage but before trial: one-quarter, reducing towards one-tenth as trial date approaches

- Day of trial or after: maximum one-tenth
Mandatory minimums: early guilty plea allows reduction below minimum by up to one-fifth.
Discount applies to the overall sentence.

⚠️ Exam trap: the first-stage discount is one-third — not one-quarter. Contesting facts in a Newton hearing and losing reduces the discount.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: GUILTY PLEA DISCOUNT · S.73 SENTENCING ACT 2020 · ONE THIRD FIRST OPPORTUNITY · ONE QUARTER · ONE TENTH AT TRIAL · NEWTON HEARING

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: What is a Newton hearing, and when is one required?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
A Newton hearing is a fact-finding hearing by a judge following a guilty plea where D disputes the prosecution's version of facts in a way material to sentence: R v Newton [1982].
Required when:
1. D has pleaded guilty
2. D disputes the factual basis in a way that would materially affect sentence
3. Prosecution does not accept D's basis of plea
If D loses: may lose some or all of the guilty plea discount.

⚠️ Not every factual dispute requires a Newton hearing — only where resolution makes a material difference to sentence.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: NEWTON HEARING · FACTUAL DISPUTE · DEFENDANT PLEADS GUILTY · PROSECUTION CONTESTS BASIS · JUDGE DECIDES FACTS · DISCOUNT REDUCED IF LOSES

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: What is the custody threshold and what are the main aggravating and mitigating factors?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Custody threshold (s.230 SA 2020): custodial sentence only where the offence was so serious that neither a fine nor community sentence can be justified.
Statutory aggravating factors:

- Previous convictions (recent and relevant)

- Offence committed on bail

- Hostility based on protected characteristics
General aggravating: planning; multiple victims; vulnerable victim; abuse of trust; leading role.
Mitigating: no previous convictions; genuine remorse; voluntary reparation; mental disorder; youth; deteriorating health.

⚠️ Custody is the sentence of last resort — crossing the threshold does not make custody mandatory.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: CUSTODY THRESHOLD · S.230 SENTENCING ACT 2020 · SO SERIOUS · AGGRAVATING FACTORS · MITIGATING FACTORS · GUIDELINES

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: How does an appeal from the magistrates' court to the Crown Court work?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Appeal to Crown Court under s.108 Magistrates' Courts Act 1980:
1. Automatic right — no leave required
2. Must be lodged within 21 days of sentence (or conviction where D pleaded not guilty)
3. Crown Court rehears the case de novo
4. Crown Court can confirm, reverse, or vary the decision
5. On sentence appeal, Crown Court can increase as well as decrease — up to the maximum the magistrates could have imposed

⚠️ Exam trap: D risks an increased sentence on appeal — always warn before advising an appeal.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: APPEAL FROM MAGISTRATES · CROWN COURT · REHEARING · CONVICTION OR SENTENCE · S.108 MCA 1980 · DE NOVO

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: What is an appeal by way of case stated from the magistrates' court?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Appeal by way of case stated to the King's Bench Division: s.111 Magistrates' Courts Act 1980. Grounds: error on a point of law or jurisdiction.
Process: applicant requests magistrates to state a case — findings of fact and the question of law. High Court may confirm, reverse, or vary, or remit with its opinion. Either party may use it.

**

**Key distinction:**** Crown Court = full rehearing de novo; case stated = pure point of law.

⚠️ Alternative: judicial review for jurisdictional errors. Both cannot be pursued simultaneously.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: CASE STATED · MAGISTRATES · HIGH COURT · QUESTION OF LAW · ERROR OF LAW OR EXCESS OF JURISDICTION · NOT FACTS

Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.front: What are the grounds and procedure for appeal from the Crown Court to the Court of Appeal?
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.answer:
Appeals — Criminal Appeal Act 1968.
Conviction: leave required. Court allows appeal if conviction is unsafe: s.2 CAA 1968. 'Unsafe' includes procedural unfairness and fresh evidence.
Sentence: leave required. Court may reduce but cannot increase D's sentence on D's own appeal.
Timing: 28 days from conviction/sentence.
Further appeal to Supreme Court: requires leave and certified point of law of general public importance.

⚠️ Exam trap: 'unsafe' is the sole test. A conviction may be unsafe on procedural grounds even where D is factually guilty.
Classification, Bail, Disclosure & Appea.summary_line: APPEAL FROM CROWN COURT · COURT OF APPEAL · S.1 CAA 1968 · UNSAFE CONVICTION · LEAVE REQUIRED · SENTENCE APPEAL

Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.front: What are the jurisdiction and sentencing powers of the youth court?
Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.answer:
Youth court: exclusive jurisdiction over defendants aged 10–17. Max custodial sentence: Detention and Training Order (DTO) of up to 24 months.
Crown Court jurisdiction for young defendants:
1. Charged with homicide — must be sent to Crown Court
2. 'Grave crime' (s.250 SA 2020) — 14+ year maximum for adult — where youth court sentencing powers insufficient
3. Jointly charged with adult sent to Crown Court: s.24 MCA 1980
Age at time of offence determines mode of trial; age at sentence determines sentencing powers.

⚠️ Exam trap: the default is always the youth court. Crown Court is an exception.
Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.summary_line: YOUTH COURT · MAGISTRATES · UNDER 18 · JURISDICTION · GRAVE CRIMES · HOMICIDE — CROWN COURT · SENTENCING POWERS

Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.front: What is the procedure for sending indictable-only offences to the Crown Court under s.51 CDA 1998?
Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.answer:
Under s.51 CDA 1998, the magistrates' court must send an adult charged with an indictable-only offence directly to the Crown Court at the first hearing. No committal or allocation hearing — magistrates have no jurisdiction to try the case.

**

**Procedure:****
1. D appears at magistrates' — first hearing only
2. Magistrates confirm charge(s) and send to Crown Court forthwith
3. Bail or custody decision made at magistrates'
4. Related either-way and summary offences may be sent alongside
5. Crown Court arraignment follows

⚠️ Exam trap: s.51 sending is automatic and immediate — no discretion.
Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.summary_line: INDICTABLE ONLY · S.51 CRIME AND DISORDER ACT 1998 · SENT TO CROWN COURT · NO COMMITTAL · FIRST HEARING AT MAGISTRATES

Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.front: When can magistrates commit a defendant to the Crown Court for sentence?
Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.answer:
Following conviction in the magistrates' court, magistrates may commit to Crown Court for sentence where:
1. Either-way offence and, having obtained more information, magistrates conclude their sentencing powers are inadequate: s.14 SA 2020.
2. D is also to be sentenced for other offences — related committal may follow.
Before accepting jurisdiction at allocation, magistrates must warn D they retain the power to commit for sentence.
Crown Court on committal: full Crown Court sentencing powers.

⚠️ Exam trap: omission of the warning does not invalidate the subsequent committal.
Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.summary_line: COMMITTAL FOR SENTENCE · S.14 SENTENCING ACT 2020 · EITHER-WAY · MAGISTRATES INSUFFICIENT POWERS · CROWN COURT SENTENCES

Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.front: What happens at allocation where co-defendants face different modes of trial?
Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.answer:
Where jointly charged and the offence is either-way:
1. If all co-defendants consent to summary trial: case proceeds in magistrates'
2. If one co-defendant elects Crown Court: magistrates have a discretion to send all defendants to Crown Court together
3. Where one defendant faces an indictable-only offence: sent under s.51 CDA 1998; either-way co-defendant charged with linked matters may be sent alongside under s.51(3)
Summary-only co-defendants: cannot be sent to Crown Court for trial — only for sentence (s.41 CDA 1998).

⚠️ Exam trap: magistrates' discretion to send an either-way co-defendant alongside does not compel joinder.
Classification, Allocation & Youth Court.summary_line: CO-DEFENDANTS · DIFFERENT MODES OF TRIAL · LINKED CASES · MAGISTRATES CAN ACCEPT JURISDICTION · CROWN COURT FOR INDICTABLE ONLY

Bail & Custody Time Limits.front: What conditions may be attached to bail, and what are the restrictions on pre-charge police bail?
Bail & Custody Time Limits.answer:
Bail conditions must be necessary to prevent the grounds for withholding bail from arising. Common conditions: residence; curfew; reporting; surrender of passport; non-contact; exclusion from areas.
Pre-charge bail — PCSC Act 2022 reforms:

- Initial: max 28 days without superintendent authorisation

- Extended: superintendent up to 3 months; senior officer up to 6 months

- Complex/serious: up to 12 months (magistrates' authorisation) or beyond

⚠️ Exam trap: the 2022 Act significantly reformed police bail timescales.
Bail & Custody Time Limits.summary_line: BAIL CONDITIONS · CURFEW · RESIDENCE · REPORTING · SURETY · ELECTRONIC MONITORING · RESTRICTIONS ON CONDITIONS

Bail & Custody Time Limits.front: What are custody time limits under s.22 CPIA 1996?
Bail & Custody Time Limits.answer:
Custody time limits (CTLs) are maximum periods D can be held in custody awaiting trial without extension.
Key periods:

- Magistrates': 56 days between first appearance and start of summary trial

- Crown Court: 182 days between sending and start of trial on indictment
Extension: prosecution must apply before expiry and show:

1. good and sufficient cause
2. prosecution has acted with all due diligence and expedition.

Consequence of expiry without extension: D must be released on bail — not released from the charge.

⚠️ Exam trap: CTL expiry means release from custody — not release from the charge.
Bail & Custody Time Limits.summary_line: CUSTODY TIME LIMITS · S.22 CPIA 1996 · 70 DAYS MAGISTRATES · 182 DAYS CROWN COURT · APPLICATION TO EXTEND · BAIL IF EXPIRED

Evidence — Hearsay, Identification & Pri.front: What are the gateways for admissibility of hearsay under the CJA 2003?
Evidence — Hearsay, Identification & Pri.answer:
Hearsay is inadmissible unless it falls within a gateway under s.114 CJA 2003:
1. s.114(1)(a): statutory provision (e.g. business records s.117; unavailable witnesses s.116)
2. s.114(1)(b): all parties agree
3. s.114(1)(c): preserved common law exception (res gestae; confession to non-party)
4. s.114(1)(d): interests of justice — discretionary gateway considering s.114(2) factors
Section 116 — unavailable witnesses: admissible where person is dead; ill; outside UK; cannot be found; or in fear.

⚠️ Exam trap: s.114(1)(d) is a residual gateway — not a free pass. The court must weigh the s.114(2) factors.
Evidence — Hearsay, Identification & Pri.summary_line: HEARSAY · CJA 2003 · FOUR GATEWAYS · STATUTORY · PRESERVED COMMON LAW · AGREEMENT · INTERESTS OF JUSTICE · S.114

Evidence — Hearsay, Identification & Pri.front: What are the Turnbull guidelines on identification evidence?
Evidence — Hearsay, Identification & Pri.answer:
The Turnbull guidelines (R v Turnbull [1977]) apply whenever a case depends wholly or substantially on disputed visual identification. The judge must:
1. Warn the jury of the special need for caution
2. Direct the jury to examine circumstances of the identification: duration; lighting; distance; familiarity with D; time between observation and identification
3. Where identification is poor: consider withdrawing the case from the jury
PACE Code D governs identification procedures (VIPER; identification parade). Breach of Code D may lead to exclusion under s.78 PACE.

⚠️ Exam trap: Turnbull applies to disputed visual identification — not voice recognition or circumstantial evidence.
Evidence — Hearsay, Identification & Pri.summary_line: TURNBULL GUIDELINES · IDENTIFICATION EVIDENCE · GOOD OR POOR CONDITIONS · SUPPORTING EVIDENCE · WARNING TO JURY · R V TURNBULL

Evidence — Hearsay, Identification & Pri.front: What is the scope of legal professional privilege in criminal proceedings?
Evidence — Hearsay, Identification & Pri.answer:
Legal professional privilege (LPP) is absolute — cannot be overridden by the court.
Two forms:
1. Legal advice privilege: communications between lawyer and client for the purpose of giving/receiving legal advice. Applies whether or not litigation is contemplated. Permanent.
2. Litigation privilege: communications between lawyer, client, and third parties made for the dominant purpose of reasonably anticipated or existing litigation. Wider scope but requires contemplated litigation.
Crime/fraud exception: LPP does not apply where the communication was made to facilitate a crime or fraud: R v Cox and Railton [1884].

⚠️

**

**Key distinction:**** legal advice privilege is narrower (lawyer-client only) but requires no pending litigation. Litigation privilege is wider but requires contemplated litigation.
Evidence — Hearsay, Identification & Pri.summary_line: LEGAL PROFESSIONAL PRIVILEGE · CRIMINAL PROCEEDINGS · ADVICE PRIVILEGE · LITIGATION PRIVILEGE · CRIME-FRAUD EXCEPTION

Identification Evidence — Key Cases.issue: What safeguards should the trial judge apply where a case depends wholly or substantially on disputed visual identification evidence?
Identification Evidence — Key Cases.rule: Where a case depends wholly or substantially on disputed visual identification evidence, the judge must warn the jury of the special need for caution and direct them to examine the circumstances of the identification. The judge should assess the quality of the identification and withdraw the case where identification is poor and uncorroborated.
Identification Evidence — Key Cases.application: D was convicted of robbery partly on disputed eyewitness identification. The CA quashed the conviction and issued guidelines: the jury must be warned of the dangers; directed to consider the quality of the observation (duration, lighting, distance, familiarity); and told to look for corroborating evidence.
Identification Evidence — Key Cases.conclusion: Turnbull is the foundational authority on identification evidence and is applied in virtually every case where visual identification is disputed. Its guidelines are supplemented by PACE Code D. Failure to give a proper Turnbull direction is a common ground of appeal.
Identification Evidence — Key Cases.summary_line: R V TURNBULL 1977 · IDENTIFICATION EVIDENCE · GUIDELINES · POOR CONDITIONS — WITHDRAW FROM JURY · SUPPORTING EVIDENCE

PACE & Evidence — Key Cases.issue: What does 'oppression' mean for the purposes of the mandatory exclusion of confessions under s.76(2)(a) PACE 1984?
PACE & Evidence — Key Cases.rule: Oppression under s.76(2)(a) bears its ordinary dictionary meaning — the exercise of power in a burdensome, harsh, or wrongful manner; unjust or cruel treatment. It imports an element of impropriety on the part of the interrogators.
PACE & Evidence — Key Cases.application: D claimed her confession should be excluded because police told her, during questioning, that her lover was having an affair with the woman in the next cell. D claimed this caused such distress that she confessed. The CA held this did not constitute oppression — though morally questionable, the police conduct was not the exercise of power in a burdensome, harsh, or wrongful manner.
PACE & Evidence — Key Cases.conclusion: Fulling sets a high threshold for oppression under s.76(2)(a). It requires improper and coercive conduct — not merely distressing circumstances. Regularly contrasted with s.76(2)(b) (unreliability), which has a lower threshold and does not require bad faith.
PACE & Evidence — Key Cases.summary_line: R V FULLING 1987 · OPPRESSION · S.76(2)(A) PACE 1984 · EXERCISE OF POWER · NARROWLY CONSTRUED · CA

PACE & Evidence — Key Cases.issue: Whether a confession obtained after police falsely told D and D's solicitor that D's fingerprint had been found at the scene should be excluded under s.78 PACE 1984.
PACE & Evidence — Key Cases.rule: The court has discretion under s.78 PACE 1984 to exclude prosecution evidence where admission would have such an adverse effect on the fairness of proceedings that the court ought not to admit it. Deliberate deception of both D and D's legal adviser is a factor strongly favouring exclusion.
PACE & Evidence — Key Cases.application: Police falsely told D and his solicitor that D's fingerprint had been found on a bottle used to start a fire. In reliance on this, D confessed. The CA held the confession should have been excluded: the police had deliberately deceived not only D but also his solicitor — undermining the fairness of the proceedings.
PACE & Evidence — Key Cases.conclusion: Mason is the leading s.78 PACE authority on deceptive police conduct. The deception of the solicitor elevated the case — it prevented D from receiving genuinely independent legal advice. Contrast with Fulling (s.76 oppression) — bad faith is not required for s.78, but deliberate deception weighs heavily.
PACE & Evidence — Key Cases.summary_line: R V MASON 1988 · S.78 PACE 1984 · DECEPTION BY POLICE · DISCRETIONARY EXCLUSION · ADVERSE EFFECT ON FAIRNESS

Scenario Cards — Criminal Procedure.front: Mike is charged with theft (an either-way offence). He appears at the magistrates' court for the first time. Walk through the allocation procedure the court must follow.
Scenario Cards — Criminal Procedure.answer:
For an either-way offence, the magistrates' court must follow the allocation procedure under ss.17A-21 Magistrates' Courts Act 1980.

**

**Step 1:**** The court considers the Allocation Guideline and decides whether its sentencing powers are sufficient given the offence's seriousness. Step 2: If the magistrates consider the case suitable for summary trial, they must offer the defendant the option of being tried in the magistrates' court or Crown Court (the defendant's election). Step 3: If Mike elects Crown Court, the case is sent there. If Mike consents to summary trial, the magistrates may try him. Step 4: If the magistrates determine the case is more suitable for the Crown Court (e.g. due to seriousness), they decline jurisdiction and send the case to the Crown Court regardless of Mike's preference. Throughout, the defendant should be warned that even if tried summarily, the court can commit to Crown Court for sentence after conviction if the offence proves more serious than first appeared.
Scenario Cards — Criminal Procedure.summary_line: EITHER-WAY OFFENCE · ALLOCATION PROCEDURE · MCA 1980 SS.17A-21 · MAGISTRATES CONSIDER SUITABILITY · DEFENDANT ELECTION · CROWN COURT SENDING · COMMIT FOR SENTENCE

Scenario Cards — Criminal Procedure.front: Nina is arrested and charged with assault. At her first hearing she applies for bail. The prosecution opposes bail on the grounds that she will fail to surrender. What must the court consider, and what conditions might be imposed if bail is granted?
Scenario Cards — Criminal Procedure.answer: Bail is governed by the Bail Act 1976. There is a general right to bail for unconvicted defendants (s.4 BA 1976), which the prosecution must rebut. The court may refuse bail only on specified grounds in Schedule 1 BA 1976, including: substantial grounds to believe D will fail to surrender, commit further offences, or interfere with witnesses. In assessing the failure to surrender ground, the court considers: nature and seriousness of the offence, character and antecedents, community ties, previous bail history, and strength of evidence. If the court is persuaded to grant bail despite the risk, it may impose conditions: residence requirements, curfews, reporting to a police station, surrender of passport, electronic monitoring (tagged bail). The court must record reasons for granting or refusing bail. Assault is not a homicide or rape offence triggering the reverse burden under s.25 CJA 1988.
Scenario Cards — Criminal Procedure.summary_line: BAIL · BAIL ACT 1976 · S.4 RIGHT TO BAIL · SCHEDULE 1 GROUNDS · FAILURE TO SURRENDER · CONDITIONS · COMMUNITY TIES · REASONS RECORDED

Scenario Cards — Criminal Procedure.front: Oscar is interviewed by police under caution. He says nothing during the interview, following his solicitor's advice. At trial, the prosecution invites the jury to draw an adverse inference from his silence. When can adverse inferences be drawn from silence at interview?
Scenario Cards — Criminal Procedure.answer:
Adverse inferences from silence at interview are governed by s.34 Criminal Justice and Public Order Act 1994. An adverse inference may be drawn where:

1. D was questioned under caution
2. D failed to mention a fact when questioned
3. D now relies on that fact in his defence at trial
4. the fact was one which, in the circumstances at the time of questioning, D could reasonably have been expected to mention.

Critically, adverse inferences cannot be drawn if D was not given the opportunity to consult a solicitor before questioning (s.34(2A) — the Condron v UK [2001] ECHR principle). On these facts, Oscar was accompanied by a solicitor and followed legal advice to remain silent — following legal advice does not automatically prevent inference, but the jury must be directed that if they accept Oscar had a genuine reason (legal advice) for silence, they may not draw an inference (R v Argent [1997]).
Scenario Cards — Criminal Procedure.summary_line: ADVERSE INFERENCE · CJPOA 1994 S.34 · SILENCE AT INTERVIEW · SOLICITOR PRESENT · CONDRON V UK · R V ARGENT · GENUINE REASON FOR SILENCE · JURY DIRECTION

Sentencing Principles & Powers.front: What are the requirements for a community sentence?
Sentencing Principles & Powers.answer:
A community order may be imposed where the offence is serious enough to warrant a community sentence: s.204 SA 2020. Must include at least one requirement from s.201 SA 2020.
Available requirements: unpaid work (40–300 hours); Rehabilitation Activity Requirement (RAR); programme requirement; curfew (up to 16 hours/day; up to 12 months); exclusion; residence; mental health, drug rehabilitation, or alcohol treatment (with consent); attendance centre (under 25).
Breach: failure without reasonable excuse may result in further sanction or resentencing — up to custody where wilfully and persistently breached.

⚠️ Exam trap: treatment requirements require D's consent. Unpaid work and other punitive requirements do not.
Sentencing Principles & Powers.summary_line: COMMUNITY SENTENCE · COMMUNITY ORDER · S.200 SENTENCING ACT 2020 · THRESHOLD · REQUIREMENTS · UNPAID WORK · CURFEW · REHABILITATION

Sentencing Principles & Powers.front: What are the dangerous offender sentencing provisions and when does an extended determinate sentence apply?
Sentencing Principles & Powers.answer:
Dangerous offender provisions (SA 2020) apply to specified sexual and violent offences (Sch.18) where D presents a significant risk of serious harm from further specified offences.
Extended determinate sentence (EDS): s.255 SA 2020 — available where:
1. D convicted of a specified offence
2. D assessed as dangerous (significant risk of serious harm — death or serious personal injury)
3. Appropriate custodial term is less than 4 years (or life not imposed)
Structure: custodial term + extended licence (up to 5 years for specified violent; up to 8 years for specified sexual). Release: must serve at least two-thirds before Parole Board may direct release.

⚠️ Exam trap: dangerousness assessment is distinct from seriousness of the current offence.
Sentencing Principles & Powers.summary_line: DANGEROUS OFFENDER · SPECIFIED OFFENCES · SIGNIFICANT RISK · EXTENDED DETERMINATE SENTENCE · S.254 SENTENCING ACT 2020

Sentencing Principles & Powers.front: What is the totality principle in sentencing?
Sentencing Principles & Powers.answer:
The totality principle requires that where a court imposes multiple sentences, the overall sentence must be just and proportionate to the totality of D's offending — it should not be crushing: Sentencing Council Guideline on Totality.
Consecutive sentences: appropriate where offences are distinct in nature, time, and impact. Total must be 'stepped back' to check proportionality.
Concurrent sentences: appropriate where offences arise out of the same incident or are closely linked.
Practical application: set sentence for each offence; then check whether consecutive total is just. If not — adjust down.

⚠️ Exam trap: concurrent vs consecutive is not a binary rule based on whether offences are on the same occasion. The question is always whether the aggregate reflects overall criminality without being crushing.
Sentencing Principles & Powers.summary_line: TOTALITY PRINCIPLE · CONSECUTIVE SENTENCES · OVERALL SENTENCE · JUST AND PROPORTIONATE · DEFINITIVE GUIDELINE

Sentencing Principles & Powers.front: What is a victim personal statement, and what role does it play in sentencing?
Sentencing Principles & Powers.answer:
A victim personal statement (VPS) is a written statement from the victim describing the impact of the offence — physical, emotional, financial, social. Governed by the Victims' Code and Criminal Practice Directions.
Role in sentencing:
1. Court must take the VPS into account
2. May be read aloud in court — a right under the Victims' Code
3. Can go to aggravating factors but cannot be used to argue for a specific sentence
4. Where harm goes beyond what D would have anticipated, the court must be cautious about elevating sentence on that basis alone

⚠️ Exam trap: the VPS does not give the victim a sentencing role. D is sentenced for the foreseeable harm — not for a worse outcome D could not have foreseen.
Sentencing Principles & Powers.summary_line: VICTIM PERSONAL STATEMENT · NOT DETERMINATIVE · JUDGE CANNOT GIVE MORE WEIGHT · SUBMITTED IN WRITING · READ ALOUD OPTION

Scenario Cards — Sentencing.front: Patrick pleads guilty to theft at the first opportunity in the magistrates' court. The magistrates are considering the sentence. How does the guilty plea affect the sentence, and what is the maximum reduction available?
Scenario Cards — Sentencing.answer: A guilty plea entitles the defendant to a reduction in sentence under the Sentencing Council's Guilty Plea Reduction guideline (effective 2017). The maximum reduction is one-third, available only where the plea is entered at the first stage of proceedings — i.e., at the first hearing at which a plea is entered. Where the first opportunity is in the magistrates' court and Patrick pleads guilty at the first hearing, he is entitled to the full one-third reduction off the sentence that would otherwise have been imposed. The reduction tapers as the case progresses: one-quarter if not at the first stage but before the trial date is set, and one-tenth on the day of trial. The reduction applies to the custodial element, fine, and community order. It does not affect any ancillary orders.
Scenario Cards — Sentencing.summary_line: GUILTY PLEA REDUCTION · ONE THIRD MAXIMUM · FIRST STAGE · SENTENCING COUNCIL GUIDELINE · TAPERING REDUCTION · CUSTODIAL AND FINE · ANCILLARY ORDERS UNAFFECTED

Scenario Cards — Sentencing.front: Rita has been convicted of a serious sexual offence. The judge considers whether to impose an extended sentence under the Criminal Justice Act 2003. What conditions must be satisfied before an extended sentence can be imposed?
Scenario Cards — Sentencing.answer:
An extended sentence under s.226A Criminal Justice Act 2003 (as amended) may be imposed where:

1. the offence is a specified offence — sexual offences are listed in Schedule 15 CJA 2003
2. the court considers there is a significant risk to members of the public of serious harm occasioned by the commission by the offender of further specified offences (the dangerousness assessment)
3. either the offender has a previous conviction for a Schedule 15B offence, or the notional determinate term would be at least 4 years.

Where conditions are met, the extended sentence consists of a custodial term plus an extension period during which the offender is on licence. The extension period is up to 5 years for sexual offences. The dangerousness assessment involves consideration of all available information about the offender, the nature of the offence, and pre-sentence reports.
Scenario Cards — Sentencing.summary_line: EXTENDED SENTENCE · CJA 2003 S.226A · SPECIFIED OFFENCE · SCHEDULE 15 · DANGEROUSNESS ASSESSMENT · SIGNIFICANT RISK · EXTENSION PERIOD · LICENCE

Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Should oblique intention under Woollin be treated as a form of intention, or merely as evidence from which a jury may find intention?
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.answer:
The Woollin direction [1999] provides that where D foresaw death or serious bodily harm as a virtual certainty (barring some unforeseen intervention) and appreciated that this was the case, the jury 'may find' — not 'must find' — intention. This permissive formulation has generated sustained academic controversy about the nature of oblique intention.

The prevailing academic view, associated with John Gardner and Tony Honoré and articulated extensively by Glanville Williams, is that the Woollin direction does not create a species of intention at all. Rather, foresight of virtual certainty is merely evidential — it is the strongest possible evidence from which a jury may infer purpose, but it does not constitute intention as a matter of substantive law. On this view, a jury that finds virtual certainty but concludes D lacked purpose should acquit of murder.

Alan Norrie takes a different position in his influential work 'Crime, Reason and History' (3rd ed., 2014), arguing that the distinction between intention and recklessness is inherently unstable and that the Woollin formulation reflects a judicial compromise that obscures rather than resolves the underlying tension. Norrie contends the 'may find' formula allows juries to import moral assessments under cover of legal doctrine.

The Law Commission in its 2006 Report (Law Com No 304, 'Murder, Manslaughter and Infanticide') recommended statutory codification of intention to include foresight of virtual certainty as a species of intention proper — which would have resolved the ambiguity in favour of the substantive view. Parliament did not act on this recommendation.

The unresolved tension is whether Woollin leaves juries with a genuine normative discretion (to find or not find intention even where virtual certainty is established) or whether the 'may find' formula is merely a linguistic concession that in practice operates as 'must find.' The Supreme Court has not definitively resolved this.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.summary_line: OBLIQUE INTENTION · WOOLLIN · VIRTUAL CERTAINTY · MAY FIND VS MUST FIND · GLANVILLE WILLIAMS · ALAN NORRIE · LAW COM NO 304 · SUBSTANTIVE VS EVIDENTIAL

Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Is the M'Naghten definition of insanity fit for purpose, and what has the Law Commission proposed?
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.answer:
The M'Naghten Rules [1843] define insanity as a defect of reason, caused by a disease of the mind, such that D either did not know the nature and quality of the act, or did not know that what they were doing was wrong. The rules were formulated in the mid-nineteenth century and have attracted extensive criticism as medically and legally outmoded.

The principal medical critique, associated with scholars including Nigel Walker in 'Crime and Insanity in England' (1968), is that the rules focus exclusively on cognitive impairment and ignore volitional impairments — a defendant who knows what they are doing and knows it is wrong but cannot control themselves due to a recognised psychiatric condition receives no protection. This excludes significant categories of serious mental disorder from the defence.

The legal critique is equally damaging: the courts have interpreted 'disease of the mind' functionally rather than medically, bringing within the insanity defence conditions such as diabetes (hyperglycaemia: R v Hennessy [1989]), epilepsy (R v Sullivan [1984]), and sleepwalking (R v Burgess [1991]). The result is that defendants with wholly physical or neurological conditions are labelled 'insane' when their condition has no psychiatric character.

The Law Commission in its 2013 Discussion Paper 'Criminal Liability: Insanity and Automatism' proposed replacing M'Naghten with a broader recognised medical condition defence that would include volitional impairment and would use medically meaningful criteria rather than the archaic 'disease of the mind' language. The 2016 Scoping Paper reaffirmed the need for reform but stopped short of a full report.

The unresolved tension is whether a reformed defence risks over-inclusion — allowing defendants with minor conditions to escape liability — or whether the current rules produce injustice in the opposite direction by criminalising the genuinely mentally ill.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.summary_line: M'NAGHTEN · INSANITY · DISEASE OF THE MIND · COGNITIVE ONLY · VOLITIONAL IMPAIRMENT EXCLUDED · NIGEL WALKER · LAW COM 2013 · RECOGNISED MEDICAL CONDITION · REFORM

Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Is R v Jogee [2016] a principled correction of parasitic accessory liability or a pragmatic retreat that leaves the law uncertain?
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.answer:
R v Jogee [2016] UKSC 8 overruled Chan Wing-Siu v R [1985] and decades of parasitic accessory liability (PAL) doctrine. Under PAL, a secondary party who foresaw that the principal might commit a further offence could be convicted of that offence — foresight of possibility was treated as sufficient mens rea. The Supreme Court held this was a wrong turn taken in 1985 and reinstated the pre-Chan Wing-Siu position: a secondary party must intend to assist or encourage the principal to commit the specific offence.

The principled case for Jogee is compelling. Jeremy Horder in 'Homicide and the Politics of Law Reform' (2012) had long argued that PAL violated the fundamental principle that liability requires a sufficiently culpable mental state in relation to the specific harm caused. Allowing foresight of mere possibility to ground murder liability treated secondary parties more harshly than principals.

However, Andrew Simester (writing in the Cambridge Law Journal, 2012) and others raised practical concerns even before Jogee about what the alternative would look like. Post-Jogee, the courts have struggled to articulate precisely what 'intention to assist' requires in gang violence contexts — does D's participation in a knife-carrying gang demonstrate intention to assist in a stabbing? The Court of Appeal's attempts to give content to the post-Jogee test (R v Johnson [2016]; R v Anwar [2016]) suggest ongoing uncertainty.

The Law Commission in Law Com No 305 (2007, 'Participating in Crime') had itself proposed a conditional intent test as an alternative — D would be liable if they intended to assist the principal in doing acts of the type committed, even if not the specific act. Parliament did not implement this and Jogee did not adopt it.

The unresolved tension is whether Jogee's restoration of full intent requirement produces acquittals in cases of genuine culpability, or whether the instinct behind PAL — that those who arm themselves together should share consequences — is morally sound but legally impermissible.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.summary_line: JOGEE · CHAN WING-SIU OVERRULED · PARASITIC ACCESSORY LIABILITY · INTENTION TO ASSIST · JEREMY HORDER · ANDREW SIMESTER · LAW COM NO 305 · CONDITIONAL INTENT

Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Should duress be available as a defence to murder? The Law Commission's position and the academic debate.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.answer:
The House of Lords in R v Howe [1987] confirmed that duress is unavailable as a defence to murder (principal or secondary), and R v Gotts [1992] extended the exclusion to attempted murder. The rationale in Howe (per Lord Hailsham) was that the law should not recognise a defence that allows one innocent life to be sacrificed to preserve another — the preciousness of human life justifies the absolute rule.

The Law Commission strongly disagreed. In Law Com No 304 (2006, 'Murder, Manslaughter and Infanticide'), the Commission recommended that duress should be available as a defence to first degree murder (in the proposed reformed structure), subject to strict conditions. The Commission's reasoning, endorsed by academic commentators including David Ormerod and Karl Laird (Smith and Hogan's Criminal Law, 15th ed.), is that the current rule produces injustice: a person who kills under a genuine, immediate threat of death to themselves or their family is arguably less culpable than a premeditated murderer, and the complete denial of the defence forces juries into perverse acquittals or compromise verdicts.

Jeremy Horder in 'Excusing Crime' (2004) offers a nuanced defence of the current position, arguing that the absence of duress for murder reflects the unique gravity of taking innocent life — a threshold beyond which compulsion cannot excuse. Horder frames this as a matter of the proper limits of excuse doctrine, not merely a policy judgment.

The counterargument from a rights-based perspective (associated with Alan Norrie) is that denying duress in extreme cases instrumentalises the defendant — requiring them to sacrifice their life or that of loved ones to preserve the law's absolute prohibition — which raises serious issues under Articles 2 and 3 ECHR.

Parliament has not acted on Law Com No 304. The current English law position remains: no duress defence to murder.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.summary_line: DURESS AND MURDER · R V HOWE · R V GOTTS · LAW COM NO 304 · DAVID ORMEROD · JEREMY HORDER EXCUSING CRIME · ECHR ARTS 2 AND 3 · NO REFORM

Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Is the Cunningham/G subjective recklessness test too lenient? The Caldwell debate and its aftermath.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.answer:
R v Cunningham [1957] established that recklessness in criminal law requires D to have foreseen the relevant risk and to have gone on to take it unreasonably — a wholly subjective test. Lord Diplock's reformulation in R v Caldwell [1982] introduced an objective limb: D was reckless if they either foresaw the risk or if an obvious risk existed that a reasonable person would have seen, regardless of whether D considered it.

Caldwell was strongly criticised in the academic literature from the outset. Glanville Williams in 'Recklessness Redefined' [1981] 40 CLJ 252 argued that Diplock's reformulation violated the principle of subjective fault by allowing conviction of defendants who genuinely did not foresee a risk — particularly defendants with limited cognitive capacity. The notorious case of Elliott v C [1983] (a 14-year-old with learning difficulties convicted of criminal damage for starting a fire she did not appreciate the danger of) crystallised the academic critique: Caldwell produced convictions that were morally indefensible.

R v G and Another [2003] overruled Caldwell for criminal damage. The House of Lords, in a speech by Lord Bingham drawing explicitly on the Law Commission's draft criminal code and academic commentary, restored the Cunningham subjective test. Lord Bingham endorsed the principle that a person should not be criminally liable for inadvertent risk-taking that they did not appreciate.

The residual academic debate concerns whether the Cunningham standard is now too permissive. Jeremy Horder has argued that some forms of inadvertent risk-taking — particularly by those in positions of responsibility — may warrant liability without subjective foresight, pointing to gross negligence manslaughter as a model. The Law Commission's draft Criminal Code had proposed different recklessness standards for different offence contexts, which Parliament has not adopted.

The unresolved question is whether a single subjective test across all criminal recklessness contexts is principled or whether context-specific standards would better reflect the varying demands of criminal culpability.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.summary_line: RECKLESSNESS · CUNNINGHAM · CALDWELL OVERRULED · R V G AND ANOTHER · GLANVILLE WILLIAMS · ELLIOTT V C · SUBJECTIVE TEST · JEREMY HORDER · LAW COMMISSION DRAFT CODE

Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Does the Ivey test for dishonesty adequately protect defendants compared to the old Ghosh test?
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.answer:
R v Ghosh [1982] set out a two-stage test for dishonesty:

1. was the conduct dishonest by the standards of ordinary decent people (objective)?
2. Did D realise it was dishonest by those standards (subjective)? The second limb — the defendant's awareness of how ordinary people would judge them — provided a subjective protection: if D genuinely did not realise their conduct was dishonest, they were not liable.

Ivey v Genting Casinos [2017] UKSC 67, decided in a civil context by the Supreme Court but making clear that Ghosh should no longer be followed in criminal cases, abolished the second limb. The Ivey test requires only:
1. what were D's actual knowledge and beliefs about the facts?
2. Was D's conduct dishonest by the standards of ordinary decent people, given those facts? The second question is wholly objective.

David Ormerod and Karl Laird in Smith and Hogan's Criminal Law (15th ed., 2018) welcomed Ivey's abolition of the Ghosh second limb, arguing it had allowed defendants to escape liability by professing eccentric moral codes — the 'Robin Hood' argument. On this view, Ivey properly places dishonesty within an objective social standard.

However, Alan Norrie and others have argued that the removal of the second Ghosh limb removes an important protection for defendants who may genuinely hold different cultural or moral frameworks about what constitutes dishonesty. Without subjective awareness, a defendant from a background where the conduct was considered normal may be convicted by reference to a standard they never internalised.

A further criticism, advanced by Simester and Sullivan ('Criminal Law: Theory and Doctrine', 7th ed.), is the constitutional legitimacy concern: Ivey was decided in a civil case by the Supreme Court and effectively overruled binding criminal authority without the procedural protection of a full criminal appeal. The courts have nonetheless applied Ivey in criminal cases without difficulty.

The unresolved tension is whether the objective Ivey standard appropriately reflects the moral basis of criminal dishonesty, or whether it imposes social norms on defendants without sufficient regard for individual culpability.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.summary_line: DISHONESTY · IVEY V GENTING · GHOSH OVERRULED · TWO-STAGE TEST ABOLISHED · DAVID ORMEROD · ALAN NORRIE · SIMESTER AND SULLIVAN · OBJECTIVE STANDARD · CULTURAL FRAMEWORK

Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Is corporate manslaughter under the Corporate Manslaughter and Corporate Homicide Act 2007 effective? An academic assessment.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.answer:
The Corporate Manslaughter and Corporate Homicide Act 2007 was designed to overcome the fundamental flaw of the common law identification doctrine, which required that a senior enough 'directing mind' of the company personally committed gross negligence manslaughter — a test that proved almost impossible to satisfy in large organisations (R v P&O European Ferries [1991]; Tesco Supermarkets v Nattrass [1972]).

The 2007 Act replaced identification with an aggregation-based test: liability attaches to the organisation where the way its activities are managed or organised by its senior management amounts to a gross breach of a relevant duty of care causing death. The theoretical advantage is that collective failures across senior management, rather than a single directing mind, can found liability.

In practice, the Act has been widely regarded as underperforming. Gary Slapper, writing in the Journal of Criminal Law (2011), noted that convictions since 2008 have been largely confined to small organisations — owner-managed companies where the directing mind and senior management collapse into a single individual (essentially replicating the old identification position in practice). Large corporations have not been successfully prosecuted.

Celia Wells in 'Corporations and Criminal Responsibility' (2nd ed., 2001, predating the Act but foundational) identified the structural problem: organisations deflect liability through diffuse decision-making precisely because no single layer of management bears sole responsibility. The 2007 Act's senior management requirement, while broader than identification, still requires proof of collective fault at a senior level — which is resistant to proof in complex corporate structures.

The exclusion of individual liability under the Act (directors and managers cannot be convicted of the CMCHA offence itself, only of separate health and safety offences) has also been criticised as weakening deterrence. The unresolved question is whether structural reform of corporate governance accountability — rather than criminal law alone — is necessary to make organisations genuinely accountable for workplace deaths.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.summary_line: CORPORATE MANSLAUGHTER · CMCHA 2007 · IDENTIFICATION DOCTRINE · GARY SLAPPER · CELIA WELLS · SENIOR MANAGEMENT REQUIREMENT · LARGE ORGANISATIONS · INDIVIDUAL LIABILITY EXCLUDED

Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Is the dangerous act test in unlawful act manslaughter from R v Church coherent, and what are its critics' objections?
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.answer:
The 'dangerous act' test from R v Church [1966] requires that the unlawful act was such that a sober and reasonable person would inevitably recognise it as carrying the risk of some physical harm to some person — not necessarily death or serious injury. The test is objective in character: what matters is not whether D foresaw harm, but whether a reasonable bystander would have.

Andrew Ashworth in 'Principles of Criminal Law' (7th ed., 2013) identifies the primary incoherence: unlawful act manslaughter requires the mens rea only of the underlying unlawful act (e.g. battery), not of harm of the gravity that actually results. A defendant who commits a minor battery and thereby (entirely unforeseeeably) causes death is convicted of manslaughter — a serious offence carrying a maximum of life imprisonment — with only the MR for the minor offence. This produces what Ashworth calls a 'constructive liability' problem: D is constructed as responsible for harm they did not intend and may not have foreseen.

Glanville Williams in 'Textbook of Criminal Law' (2nd ed., 1983) pressed the same objection more forcefully, arguing that unlawful act manslaughter is essentially a crime of bad luck — two defendants with identical intent and conduct may be treated very differently depending on whether death results. The absence of correspondence between the required MR and the resulting harm violates what Williams called the 'correspondence principle.'

The courts have defended the doctrine on deterrence and fair labelling grounds: those who commit unlawful acts take the risk of their consequences, and death resulting from violence should be labelled as more serious than the underlying violence alone. The Law Commission in Consultation Paper No 177 (2005) proposed abolishing unlawful act manslaughter and replacing it with a 'criminal act manslaughter' requiring foresight of serious harm — Parliament has not acted.

The unresolved tension is between constructive liability as a deterrent and the principle that criminal liability should track individual culpability.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.summary_line: UNLAWFUL ACT MANSLAUGHTER · R V CHURCH · DANGEROUS ACT TEST · ANDREW ASHWORTH · GLANVILLE WILLIAMS · CORRESPONDENCE PRINCIPLE · CONSTRUCTIVE LIABILITY · LAW COM CP 177

Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Should consent be a defence to serious bodily harm? R v Brown [1994] and the ECHR dimension.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.answer:
R v Brown [1994] held by a 3-2 majority in the House of Lords that consent is not a defence to the deliberate infliction of actual bodily harm or above in the context of sado-masochistic activities between adults in private, on public policy grounds. The majority (Lords Templeman, Jauncey, and Lowry) held that the satisfying of sexual desire did not fall within any recognised exception to the principle that consent cannot sanction serious harm.

The minority dissent (Lords Mustill and Slynn) argued that private consensual activities between adults should not attract criminal sanction in the absence of public harm — a position associated with the liberal harm principle derived from J.S. Mill's 'On Liberty.' Lords Mustill emphasised that the activities, though distasteful to many, caused no harm to anyone who had not chosen to participate.

The ECHR dimension was litigated in Laskey, Jaggard and Brown v United Kingdom [1997]. The European Court of Human Rights upheld the UK's approach, finding that the interference with the defendants' Article 8 rights (private life) was justified under Article 8(2) as necessary in a democratic society for the protection of health — a wide margin of appreciation was accorded to the UK. This ECHR endorsement significantly limited the human rights challenge.

Yet the academic literature remains critical of Brown. Michael Allen in 'Textbook on Criminal Law' (15th ed., 2019) contrasts the outcome with cases such as R v Wilson [1997] (branding wife's initials — permitted as similar to tattooing) and R v Emmett [1999] (similar activities: convicted), suggesting the line drawn by the courts is inconsistent and arguably homophobic in origin given that Brown involved gay men.

The unresolved tension concerns the proper scope of the criminal law's role in policing private consensual harm, and whether the current position can survive sustained rights-based challenge in an era of greater recognition of individual autonomy.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.summary_line: CONSENT · R V BROWN · SADO-MASOCHISM · ABH AND ABOVE · LASKEY V UK · ECHR ART 8 · LORD MUSTILL DISSENT · R V WILSON · MICHAEL ALLEN · HARM PRINCIPLE · AUTONOMY

Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.front: Going deeper: Is the parasitic accessory liability doctrine post-Jogee still uncertain, and what unresolved questions remain?
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.answer:
R v Jogee [2016] UKSC 8 was presented as a return to orthodox secondary liability principles, but the academic and judicial reception has exposed significant remaining uncertainty.

The most immediate doctrinal question concerns conditional intent. Post-Jogee, a secondary party must intend to assist or encourage the principal to commit the specific offence. But if D participates in a joint enterprise knowing that P may use violence if needed, does D's participation constitute an intention to assist in that use of violence? The Court of Appeal in R v Johnson [2016] suggested that conditional intent to assist (if needed) is sufficient — which partly restores the practical effect of pre-Jogee law without restoring its doctrinal basis.

Jeremy Horder and David Hughes, writing in the Criminal Law Review (2016), argue that Jogee's use of the term 'intent' is insufficiently analysed — the Supreme Court did not distinguish between direct intent, oblique intent, and conditional intent in the accessory context. This leaves lower courts without clear guidance on how to direct juries.

Andrew Simester in 'Accessorial Liability after Jogee' [2017] CLR 3 raises a more fundamental objection: the orthodox intention requirement may be too demanding in gang contexts, producing acquittals where D has manifested sufficient culpability through participation. Simester advocates for a reformed 'reckless assistance' standard — which the Law Commission had included in Law Com No 305 (2007) but which Parliament and Jogee declined to adopt.

The question of how to assess D's intent at trial — particularly in cases of rapid, spontaneous violence where pre-planning is absent — has also generated inconsistent Court of Appeal decisions post-Jogee.

The unresolved tension is whether Jogee's corrective orthodoxy has produced a principled but practically unworkable standard, or whether the post-Jogee case law will eventually settle into a coherent conditional intent doctrine.
Criminal Law & Practice — Academic Depth.summary_line: JOGEE · PARASITIC ACCESSORY LIABILITY · CONDITIONAL INTENT · R V JOHNSON · JEREMY HORDER · ANDREW SIMESTER · LAW COM NO 305 · RECKLESS ASSISTANCE · UNRESOLVED

Criminal Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Alex fires a gun at a wall during an argument to scare Ben, knowing the bullet will ricochet close to Ben. The bullet kills Ben. Alex says he did not intend to kill Ben — he only intended to frighten him. He accepts he foresaw death as a virtual certainty from the ricochet. The judge directs the jury that they must find intention if they are satisfied Alex foresaw death as virtually certain.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: The judge's direction is correct — foresight of virtual certainty equals intention, so the jury must find intention and must convict of murder.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: The Woollin [1999] direction is permissive, not mandatory. Where D foresees death or GBH as a virtual certainty, the jury may find intention — but is not compelled to. The direction 'must find' overstates the law and is a misdirection.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario presents foresight of virtual certainty as automatically equivalent to intention. This is the most common misstatement of Woollin. The House of Lords in Woollin deliberately used 'may find' — preserving a residual jury discretion — rather than 'must find.' A judge who directs 'must find' misdirects the jury, potentially vitiating the conviction.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Woollin foresight of virtual certainty gives the jury the option to find intention — it does not compel it. Always use 'may find', never 'must find'.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: WOOLLIN · OBLIQUE INTENTION · VIRTUAL CERTAINTY · MAY FIND NOT MUST FIND · JURY DISCRETION · MISDIRECTION

Criminal Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Carla is an insulin-dependent diabetic. She takes her insulin but then fails to eat, causing her blood sugar to drop severely. In a hypoglycaemic state she attacks her carer David, causing him serious injury. She has no memory of the incident. Her solicitor advises her to raise insanity on the basis that her diabetic condition is a 'disease of the mind.'
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: Carla's diabetes is a recognised medical condition that caused involuntary conduct — this is a disease of the mind, so insanity is the correct defence.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: The correct defence is sane automatism, not insanity. The internal/external cause distinction determines which applies. Hypoglycaemia caused by insulin (an external agent) is an external cause — this is automatism (R v Quick [1973]). Hyperglycaemia caused by the diabetic condition itself (internal cause) is insanity (R v Hennessy [1989]). The source of the incapacity — external drug, not internal disease — determines the defence.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario emphasises that Carla has a diabetic condition and acted involuntarily, which triggers the instinct to apply insanity as a 'disease of the mind.' But the key fact is that the hypoglycaemia was caused by insulin — an external substance administered to her. Quick established that externally-caused automatism is sane automatism, producing an outright acquittal rather than the special verdict of not guilty by reason of insanity.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Insulin-induced hypoglycaemia → external cause → sane automatism (Quick). Failure to take insulin causing hyperglycaemia → internal cause → insanity (Hennessy). The agent causing the loss of control determines the defence.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: INSANITY VS AUTOMATISM · INTERNAL EXTERNAL CAUSE · R V QUICK · R V HENNESSY · INSULIN = EXTERNAL · HYPOGLYCAEMIA = AUTOMATISM · HYPERGLYCAEMIA = INSANITY

Criminal Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Ella gets extremely drunk and punches Felix in an unprovoked attack, breaking his cheekbone. She is charged with assault occasioning actual bodily harm under s.47 OAPA 1861. Her barrister argues that because of her intoxicated state, she did not form the mens rea for the offence and should be acquitted.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: If Ella did not actually form the mens rea for the assault due to her intoxication, she lacks the required mental element and should be acquitted — intoxication negatived her MR.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: Voluntary intoxication is not a defence to offences of basic intent, even if it actually prevented formation of the mens rea. Under DPP v Majewski [1977], becoming voluntarily intoxicated is itself a reckless act sufficient to supply the MR for basic intent offences. ABH under s.47 is a basic intent offence. Ella cannot rely on intoxication to negate MR.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The argument sounds logically compelling — if Ella genuinely lacked MR, how can she be convicted? But Majewski creates a legal fiction for basic intent offences: the recklessness of getting drunk is treated as the recklessness required by the offence. The defence of intoxication negativing MR is reserved for specific intent offences (e.g. s.18 GBH, murder, theft) where the higher MR of intention cannot be imputed through Majewski.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: Voluntary intoxication is only a defence to specific intent offences. For basic intent offences, becoming intoxicated supplies the recklessness required — Majewski. ABH (s.47), s.20 GBH, and manslaughter are all basic intent offences.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: VOLUNTARY INTOXICATION · MAJEWSKI · BASIC INTENT · SPECIFIC INTENT · S.47 ABH · RECKLESSNESS IMPUTED · NO DEFENCE TO BASIC INTENT · MR FICTION

Criminal Law & Practice Traps.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

George is a householder who wakes at 3am to find an intruder, Harry, in his bedroom. In a panic, George grabs a cricket bat and strikes Harry repeatedly, causing serious injuries. George is charged with GBH. He raises self-defence. The prosecution argues George used grossly disproportionate force and that this is an absolute bar to self-defence for householders.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.issue: The Trap: Grossly disproportionate force is an absolute bar to self-defence — if the force used was grossly disproportionate, no defence is available regardless of circumstances.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.rule: The Reality: For householders defending against intruders, the standard is modified by s.76(5A) Criminal Justice and Immigration Act 2008 (inserted by the Crime and Courts Act 2013). Grossly disproportionate force remains outside the defence, but force that is disproportionate (but not grossly so) may be regarded as reasonable in the circumstances. The householder provision creates a more generous test than the standard self-defence test — not an absolute bar at the 'grossly disproportionate' threshold.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The scenario assumes that 'grossly disproportionate force' means automatic failure of self-defence for everyone. The standard self-defence test under s.76(6) CJIA 2008 does treat disproportionate force as outside the defence. But the householder provision (s.76(5A)) specifically allows that a householder's disproportionate (though not grossly disproportionate) force may still be regarded as reasonable. George is a householder and Harry is an intruder — the modified test applies.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.conclusion: The Key Rule: For householders (s.76(5A) CJIA 2008): grossly disproportionate force still fails the defence, but merely disproportionate force may succeed. The householder test is more generous than standard self-defence.
Criminal Law & Practice Traps.summary_line: SELF-DEFENCE · HOUSEHOLDER · S.76(5A) CJIA 2008 · GROSSLY DISPROPORTIONATE = ABSOLUTE BAR · DISPROPORTIONATE MAY STILL SUCCEED · MODIFIED TEST · CRIME AND COURTS ACT 2013
